EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front approaching northwest New Jersey helped generate precipitation on the 20th. While it was warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, some surface temperatures were still below freezing over the higher terrain. Freezing rain fell from about 2 a.m. EST through 7 a.m. EST on the 20th before temperatures rose above freezing. Ice accretions were less than one-tenth of an inch and untreated roadways were slippery.
Lightning struck and badly damaged a home in Colts Neck Township. There were no reported injuries.
An upper air disturbance in the general northwest flow coupled with daytime heating behind the surface cold front to produce severe weather in Hunterdon and Warren Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail as large as golf balls was reported by the Phillipsburg Police.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance in the general northwest flow coupled with daytime heating behind the surface cold front to produce severe weather in Hunterdon and Warren Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail fell in Ringoes (East Amwell Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a large tree and six poles on Mills Street in Morristown. One vehicle was damaged and two blocks were closed. Power was restored on the morning of the 12th.

An eight-year-old boy drowned while playing in the ocean during the evening of the 24th after getting caught in a rip current just north of the Newark Avenue Jetty in Bradley Beach Borough. He was in waist high water when he was swept away. A woman alerted lifeguards who were participating in a tournament about 300 yards away from where the biy became trapped by the rip current. They formed a human chain and his body was pulled from the ocean approximately 30 minutes after the search began.
A strong thunderstorm affected the Tuckerton area and knocked down a gazebo. The fractured debris tore down some wires. The winds also pulled down some small tree branches.

Heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding in and around Spotswood Borough during the early afternoon on the 29th. The Shoprite Shopping Center parking lot filled with up to four feet of water and trapped several vehicles. In New Brunswick, the ramp to New Jersey State Route 18 was closed. Cars stalled in standing water along the East Brunswick and Milltown Border. Doppler Radar storm total estimates were between 1 and 2 inches.
About 11 people were rescued by lifeguards from a rip current that formed near a jetty in Belmar on the morning of the 11th. The prevailing winds were from the northeast.
A lightning strike fried the electrical wiring of a home in Lawrence Township. The lightning initially struck the air conditioning unit and then traveled through the house's wiring.

Rip currents that were enhanced by a high pressure system that moved across New England and the nearby coastal waters forced water rescues on the 18th and 19th. On the 18th, about eight people were rescued overall from Ocean County beaches. A 26-year-old woman was rescued by two teenagers off the 4th Avenue Beach in Asbury Park on the 19th.

On the 18th, about eight people were rescued overall from Ocean County beaches. A 26-year-old woman was rescued by two teenagers off the 4th Avenue Beach in Asbury Park on the 19th.

Snow showers that occurred preceding a cold front helped produce slippery conditions and accidents during the morning commute across Northwest New Jersey. Reported accumulations were an inch or less. But, the lack of snow so far this winter, the change in road conditions (since the snow showers did not affect all areas) and the confluence with the morning commute contributed to an abnormal increase in the number of accidents.| |Actual accumulations included 1.0 inch in Marcella (Morris County) and Newton (Sussex County), 0.9 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 0.8 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 0.5 inches in Hackettstown and Stewartsville (Warren County) and 0.3 inches in Riegelsville (Hunterdon County).

A persistent northwest to west flow following the winter storm on February 13th and 14th culminated in blow-out tides around the times of low tide from the late morning of the 16th into the early morning of the 17th. The lowest tides in northern New Jersey occurred around Noon EST on the 16th, and in southern New Jersey and in Delaware Bay early on the 17th. The new moon assisted, because spring tides cause the biggest differences in water levels between high and low tides. The low tide at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) reached 2.67 feet below mean lower low water; at Atlantic City (Atlantic County) reached 2.58 feet below mean lower low water and in Cape May (Cape May County) reached 2.37 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water. The west flow subsided late in the day on the 17th and on the 18th.

A renewed northwest flow coupled with the astronomical spring (low) tides around the new moon caused blow-out tides around the times of the afternoon low tide on the 19th. The low tide at both Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and Atlantic City (Atlantic County) reached 2.19 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water. Unlike previous blow-out tides this month, the northwest flow diminished quickly and was only a one low tide cycle event.

The combination of fresh water runoff and a wind flow that was upriver caused minor tidal flooding along the Delaware River and tidal sections of its tributaries during the mid-day high tide on the 1st. The high tide reached 8.29 feet above mean lower low water in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania. Minor tidal flooding starts at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.

A passing cold front produced showers and thunderstorms ahead of it, producing heavy rainfall across Northeast New Jersey. Rainfall amounts ranged from around 0.75 to just under 2.25 inches across the region, producing areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous water rescues from flooded streets.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous water rescues from flooded streets and underpasses.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A passing cold front spawned isolated Severe Thunderstorms across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported by the general public.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck in the middle of a field at a farm in Lambertville and ignited a hay fire.

A small wildfire in the Leonardo section of Middletown (Monmouth County) just east of the Earle Naval Weapons Station threatened several homes near the field during the afternoon of the 6th. About 10 acres of weeds burned in a field near the naval station. It was initially believed that the houses bordering the field would be damaged, but no property damage occurred. It took a little over an hour for the fire to be extinguished. The recent dry weather made it easier for fires to spread. Winds that afternoon from Newark International Airport were from the east at 10 to 20 mph.

Thunderstorms with torrential downpours caused poor drainage and stream flooding in the southern part of Warren County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around four inches in Franklin, Greenwich, Harmony and Lopatcong Townships.

A wildfire burned about 50 acres of Peaslee Wildlife Management Area between Hesstown Road and MacDonald Avenue in Maurice River Township during thr afternoon and evening of the 9th. The fire started about 5 p.m. EDT. It took about 20 to 25 firefighters to get the fire under control at about 10 p.m. EDT. Smoke from the fire could be seen as far as U.S. Route 40 in Atlantic County.

A lightning strike damaged a home in Hopewell Township on Caroline Drive during the early morning of the 27th. Odors of smoke and natural gas filled the home. The lightning strike apparently struck the home's natural gas meter. It traveled along the natural gas line into the laundry room (the dryer) and arced between that pipe and a water pipe. The arc generated so much heat that it soldering on both pipes melted and allowed natural gas and water to flow. The lightning also caused extensive damage to the home's electrical system. but no fire occurred.

The combination of the leftover onshore flow from the departing nor'easter and the astronomical spring tides caused by the new moon caused tidal flooding primarily from the evening high tide on the 17th through the evening high tide on the 19th. Some moderate tidal flooding occurred during the evening high tide on the 18th. The highest tides included 7.81 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 7.77 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.87 feet above mean lower low water in Atlantic City (Atlantic County). Moderate tidal flooding begins at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water and minor tidal flooding begins at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.

The remnants of Hurricane Noel caused strong winds, minor tidal flooding and beach erosion along the New Jersey coast during the day on November 3rd. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph. Minor tidal flooding occurred with the afternoon high tide including in some of the back bays. Fortunately by the afternoon winds were already shifting to the northwest. ||In Middlesex County, in Old Bridge a four foot high dune was cut at its base for one-quarter of a mile from the municipal building to the police station. In Monmouth County, in Sea Bright, snow fencing was damaged. Two foot vertical cuts occurred at the toe of the dune. The rest of the county reported vertical cuts of one foot or less. In Ocean County, most of the reported erosion occurred in Long Beach Township where vertical cuts reached between two and four feet. In the Holgate section of the township, a four foot vertical cut by a forty foot wide cut exposed concrete at the south end that led to the Forsythe National Wildlife Refuge. Elsewhere in the county, at Harvey Cedars, vertical cuts averaged three feet and sand was washed away from the retaining wall and exposed rocks on the beach at East Cumberland Avenue. Little if any erosion was reported in Atlantic County. In Cape May County, vertical cuts averaged from one to four feet. In Ocean City, the Delancy Street outfall pipe was in disrepair. In Strathmere, sand fencing was damaged. Off Sea View Avenue, a 10 foot vertical cliff was carved and a residential bulkhead was exposed. In Avalon, from 9th through 20th Streets, four foot vertical cuts to the top of the bulkhead caused rock revetment and outfall to be exposed. ||The high tide in Cape May (Cape May County) reached 6.79 feet above mean lower low water. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.70 feet above mean lower low water. Peak wind gusts included 47 mph at Barnegat Inlet (Ocean County) and 45 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County).||Noel and what became its remnants moved north from well east of the Florida Coast on the morning of the 2nd to about 250 miles east of the Delmarva Peninsula on the morning of the 3rd and made landfall in Nova Scotia overnight on the 3rd.

A low pressure system that passed just south of Lakes Michigan and Erie helped produce the first measurable snow of the winter season for the higher terrain of northwest New Jersey. Accumulations reached around four inches in far northwest Sussex County. Rain began during the afternoon of the 9th and precipitation changed to snow over the higher terrain during the late evening of the 9th. Light snow continued through the night and ended around sunrise on the morning of the 10th. Specific accumulations included 4.3 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 3.5 inches in Barry Lakes (Sussex County), 1.4 inches in Sussex (Sussex County) and 0.5 inches in both Butler and Marcella in Morris County. The low pressure system moved from Ohio on the morning of the 9th into western Pennsylvania on the evening of the 9th. A second low pressure system formed well east of the Delmarva Peninsula the same evening. The primary low pressure system stalled and dissipated in western Pennsylvania overnight as the secondary low slowly intensified and moved farther offshore. By the morning of the 10th, this low pressure system was well southeast of Cape Cod, Massachusetts.

Dense fog formed around the local Philadelphia area from the morning into the evening of the 20th. Widespread visibilities were one quarter of a mile or less. The fog formed in advance of a warm front and was assisted by the rain that fell during the day. The fog started to dissipate in the evening as the warm front moved through. At nearby Philadelphia International Airport, the dense fog caused delays to reach up to two hours on departures and up to three hours on arrivals. Only a few flights overall were canceled.

An area of low pressure moved from the northern Plains on November 17th toward the Mid Atlantic States on the 18th, with a warm front draped from Ohio to southern Delaware. Another area of low pressure however developed off the coast of Virginia during the 18th and then moved out to sea on the 19th and into the 20th. A surface trough however bridged the two areas of low pressure. Meanwhile, an upper-level low tracked across the upper Ohio Valley and right across Pennsylvania late on the 19th, however it was weakening with time. This brought accumulating snowfall to parts of northern New Jersey. This storm was elevation dependent, as temperatures in the lower elevations were above freezing by a couple of degrees during most of the event. The storm system gradually moved off to the east on the 19th, bringing an end to the widespread precipitation.| |The steady snow began falling over the higher terrain of northwest New Jersey around Midnight EST on the 19th. The snow ended during the late morning on the 19th. Accumulations were elevation dependent from Hunterdon and Somerset Counties northwest and ranged from none in the valleys to around six inches in the higher terrain.| |Specific accumulations included 6.4 inches in Marcella (Morris County), 6.0 inches in Chester (Morris County) and Tewksbury (Hunterdon County), 5.2 inches in Newton (Sussex County), 4.8 inches in Wantage (Sussex County), 4.5 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 4.0 inches in Millington (Morris County), 3.0 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 2.0 inches in Butler (Morris County), 1.2 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County), 1.1 inches in Sussex (Sussex County), 0.9 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County) and a trace in Flemington (Hunterdon County).

In the Holgate section of the township, a four foot vertical cut by a forty foot wide cut exposed concrete at the south end that led to the Forsythe National Wildlife Refuge. Elsewhere in the county, at Harvey Cedars, vertical cuts averaged three feet and sand was washed away from the retaining wall and exposed rocks on the beach at East Cumberland Avenue. Little if any erosion was reported in Atlantic County. In Cape May County, vertical cuts averaged from one to four feet. In Ocean City, the Delancy Street outfall pipe was in disrepair. In Strathmere, sand fencing was damaged. Off Sea View Avenue, a 10 foot vertical cliff was carved and a residential bulkhead was exposed. In Avalon, from 9th through 20th Streets, four foot vertical cuts to the top of the bulkhead caused rock revetment and outfall to be exposed. ||The high tide in Cape May (Cape May County) reached 6.79 feet above mean lower low water. Strong winds buffeted New Jersey during the second half of the morning and into the evening on the 3rd. Highest wind gusts averaged 50 to 55 mph and downed trees, tree limbs and caused scattered power outages. Garbage cans and tree limbs littered county roadways. The strong west winds caused water from Barnegat Bay to be spewed onto Bayview Avenue in Seaside Park (Ocean County). There was a motorcycle and trailer ban on the length of the New Jersey Turnpike.||Specific wind gusts included 55 mph in Cape May Harbor (Cape May County), 54 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 53 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), 49 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 48 mph in Millville (Cumberland County), 47 mph in Wrightstown (Burlington County), 44 mph in Trenton (Mercer County) and 43 mph in Deans (Middlesex County). The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system that was intensifying as it was moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building toward the northeast from the Southern Plains.

A weak low pressure system combined with cold air near the surface to cause freezing rain across parts of northwest New Jersey from the late afternoon into the evening of the 9th. Ice accretions averaged around one tenth of an inch over the higher terrain of Warren and Morris Counties and up to one-quarter of an inch in Sussex County. ||As precipitation started during the afternoon of the 9th in Sussex County, it began as freezing rain. In Morris and Warren Counties, the freezing rain was mainly confined to the higher terrain. Precipitation ended by midnight EST that evening.| |A large high pressure system that was over New York State and New England on the 9th supplied the dense, cold air near the surface. That morning a low pressure system started to develop along the stationary front in the Tennessee Valley. It supplied the overrunning moisture. While it was warm enough above the surface for precipitation to fall as rain, the cold dense air near the ground was tough to displace and the rain froze on contact. The low pressure system itself moved northeast and during the early evening on the 9th was near Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania. A secondary low pressure system formed along its warm frontal boundary east of Delaware overnight. By sunrise on the 10th, the low pressure system was already well east (about 150 miles) of the New Jersey coast.

Following a storm system on December 13th, a moist air mass was left in place. As a weak area of high pressure settled in early on the 14th, this allowed for light winds which combined with the moist air mass. As a result, areas of dense fog formed across southern parts of New Jersey. The dense fog occurred from about 4 AM EST and lasted until about 11 AM EST. The dense fog caused flight delays at nearby Philadelphia International Airport.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region, which is known as cold air damming. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. This produced a strong onshore flow for a time on the 16th, which resulted in some areas of beach erosion and minor coastal flooding around Noon EST at the neap tides. Some roads were closed along the Monmouth County coast at the times of high tide. The storm system moved away from the area during the overnight hours of the 16th. No additional tidal flooding occurred with subsequent high tides.||In Monmouth County, minor beach erosion (1 to 3 foot vertical cuts) occurred in Sea Bright and from Ocean Grove south through Manasquan. In Ocean County, erosion was reported in Harvey Cedars, Surf City and Holgate. Harvey Cedars reported the worst erosion with vertical cuts reaching 3 to 4 feet. No beach erosion was reported in Atlantic County. In Cape May County, minor erosion (1 to 2 foot vertical cuts) were reported around Cape May City and on the Delaware Bay side of the county.||The high tides reached 7.40 feet above mean lower low at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 6.72 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County). Minor flooding was reported along Reeds Beach in Middle Township (Cape May County). Minor tidal flooding begins at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.

A strong area of high pressure over far northern New England during the morning of December 21st, slowly moved eastward during the 22nd. Meanwhile, a storm system quickly moved off the Southeastern United States coast and out to sea. This setup allowed for a fairly prolonged period of onshore winds, which then turned to the south or southeast late on the 22nd or early on the 23rd. The combination of the persistent onshore flow, coupled with the full moon, produced areas of minor tidal flooding with the morning high tides on the 22nd and 23rd. The most widespread and highest tidal flooding occurred with the morning high tide on the 22nd. Highest tides included 7.07 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.78 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.

Highest wind gusts averaged 50 to 55 mph and downed trees, tree limbs and caused scattered power outages. Garbage cans and tree limbs littered county roadways. The strong west winds caused water from Barnegat Bay to be spewed onto Bayview Avenue in Seaside Park (Ocean County). There was a motorcycle and trailer ban on the length of the New Jersey Turnpike.||Specific wind gusts included 55 mph in Cape May Harbor (Cape May County), 54 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 53 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), 49 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 48 mph in Millville (Cumberland County), 47 mph in Wrightstown (Burlington County), 44 mph in Trenton (Mercer County) and 43 mph in Deans (Middlesex County). The strong winds were caused by the pressure difference between a low pressure system that was intensifying as it was moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building toward the northeast from the Southern Plains.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. Strong winds resulted as the storm intensified, mostly as the storm itself began to depart during the evening and overnight on the 16th. Peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph inland and around 55 mph along the immediate coast.||Measured wind gusts included 59 mph at the Barnegat Lighthouse and Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 57 mph at Keansburg (Monmouth County), Bivalve (Cumberland County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 53 mph in Toms River (Ocean County), 49 mph at the McGuire Air Force Base (Burlington County), 48 mph in Mullica (Gloucester County), 46 mph in Millville (Cumberland County), Wantage (Sussex County), Cream Ridge (Monmouth County) and Trenton (Mercer County) and 44 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), Woodstown (Salem County) and Pittstown (Hunterdon County).

An Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped two to four inches of snow across southern New Jersey and an inch or less of snow across northern New Jersey on the 5th. Snow began during the late morning and ended during the evening on the 5th. While the snow was never heavy, most of the accumulating snow across southern New Jersey occurred during two spurts, one in the early afternoon and the second during the early evening. The highest accumulating snows occurred in a band across Salem, Cumberland, Gloucester and Atlantic Counties.||Specific accumulations included 4.4 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), 4.0 inches in Rio Grande (Cape May County) and North Vineland (Cumberland County), 3.8 inches in Pittsgrove (Salem County), 3.7 inches in Cape May City (Cape May County), 3.6 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 3.5 inches in Newport (Cumberland County) and Glassboro (Gloucester County), 3.2 inches in Hammonton (Atlantic County), 3.1 inches in Quinton (Salem County) and Absecon (Atlantic County), 3.0 inches in Atsion (Burlington County), 2.5 inches in Avalon (Cape May County) and Pennsville (Salem County), 2.0 inches in Manahawkin (Ocean County), 1.6 inches in Somerdale (Camden County) and 1.5 inches in Maple Shade (Burlington County).||As the name implies, the low pressure that caused the snow moved quickly from southern Alberta at 7 a.m. EST on the 4th to the central Ohio Valley at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th. By sunrise on the 6th, it was already well east of the Virginia coast.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region, which is known as cold air damming. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. As a result, moisture was lifted up and over colder air trapped at the surface. This produced about 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet across northern New Jersey, with up to a quarter of an inch of ice (from freezing rain). The storm system moved away from the area during the overnight hours of the 16th.||The combination of snow and mostly sleet made roadways rather slippery. In Union Township (Hunterdon County), 12 people were injured when a chartered bus carrying 31 passengers to New York City crashed, early in the morning on the 16th, into a jack-knifed tractor-trailer in the eastbound lanes of Interstate 78 near exit 15. The bus struck the truck, then crashed through the right guardrail and slid about 50 feet down a ravine. In Morris County, an icy ramp in Parsippany from eastbound Interstate 80 to eastbound Interstate 280, caused a series of vehicle accidents that closed the ramp at about 1 PM EST on the 16th. Numerous spin-outs and several accidents were reported across northern New Jersey. The ice build-up on trees and power lines resulted in power outages, which were reported in Phillipsburg (Warren County) and in 7 municipalities in Hunterdon County. Because of the ice, trees and wires were downed across parts of Hunterdon and Somerset Counties. There was an incident of a vehicle windshield being smashed out on Interstate 80 from ice flying off an SUV.||While there was some snow, sleet was the dominate precipitation type. Some total accumulations included 3.0 inches in Tewksbury (Hunterdon County); 2.5 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County); 2.1 inches in Wantage (Sussex County); 1.8 inches in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), Blairstown (Warren County) and Sussex (Sussex County); 1.6 inches in Lake Parsippany (Morris County), Marcella (Morris County) and Bridgewater (Somerset County); 1.4 inches in Riegelsville (Warren County); 1.2 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County); and 1.0 inch in Flemington (Hunterdon County) and Whippany (Morris County). Ice accumulations from freezing rain included 0.25 of an inch in Tewksbury (Hunterdon County); 0.20 of an inch in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County) and Wantage (Sussex County); and 0.10 of an inch in Edison (Middlesex County).

A wintry mix of precipitation fell across central and northwestern New Jersey from the early morning of the 2nd into the early morning of the 3rd. While precipitation changed to rain by the evening of the 2nd across much of the state, freezing rain persisted through the end of the event in Sussex County. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged one to three inches across the northern third of the state and an inch or less across the central third of the state. Ice accretions averaged one tenth to one-quarter of an inch with the highest amounts in Sussex County. Many traffic accidents were caused by this wintry mix.||Precipitation started as light snow from central New Jersey north during the early morning of the 2nd. South of Interstate 195 the snow ended around dawn. When precipitation moved back later that morning, it started as freezing rain and sleet. Because of the recent warm weather and indirect insolation from the sun, roads remained wet and the freezing rain was limited to exposed surfaces. Farther to the north in the state precipitation mixed with and then changed to freezing rain from the late morning into the afternoon.  As warmer air moved inland, the freezing rain changed to rain during the late afternoon in southern New Jersey, during the evening in central New Jersey and took to about Midnight EST on the 3rd for the change to plain rain to occur in most of northwest New Jersey. Precipitation remained freezing rain over most of Sussex County until the event ended around 3 a.m. EST on the 3rd.||In Ocean County, the light snow caused a seven vehicle pile-up near Stafford Township on the Garden State Parkway.  No serious injuries occurred, but the northbound lanes were closed for one hour. In Middlesex County, the freezing rain led to a fourteen vehicle pile-up on New Jersey State Route 18 between East Brunswick and Old Bridge. Four or five separate accidents merged into one.  No serious injuries were reported, but another accident caused an injury in Old Bridge. Farther northwest in New Jersey, several singular vehicle accidents especially on Interstate 80 occurred. The wintry weather forced the cancellation of the Christmas on the Farm Celebration in Wantage Township (Sussex County).| |Specific snow and sleet accumulations included 3.1 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 2.5 inches in Sussex and Wantage (Sussex County),  2.2 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 1.9 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County), 1.5 inches in Riegelsville (Hunterdon County) and Washington Crossing (Mercer County), 1.2 inches in Edison (Middlesex County), 1.0 inch in Colts Neck (Monmouth County) and Boonton (Morris County) and 0.5 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County). ||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Great Lakes on the morning of the 2nd into the Saint Lawrence Valley on the morning of the 3rd. Even though the low pressure system moved north of the region, the cold air near the ground was difficult to dislodge. While it became warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, it still froze on contact especially over Sussex County.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region, which is known as cold air damming. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. This produced a strong onshore flow for a time on the 16th, which resulted in some areas of beach erosion and minor coastal flooding around Noon EST at the neap tides. Some roads were closed along the Monmouth County coast at the times of high tide. The storm system moved away from the area during the overnight hours of the 16th. No additional tidal flooding occurred with subsequent high tides.||In Monmouth County, minor beach erosion (1 to 3 foot vertical cuts) occurred in Sea Bright and from Ocean Grove south through Manasquan. In Ocean County, erosion was reported in Harvey Cedars, Surf City and Holgate. Harvey Cedars reported the worst erosion with vertical cuts reaching 3 to 4 feet. No beach erosion was reported in Atlantic County. In Cape May County, minor erosion (1 to 2 foot vertical cuts) were reported around Cape May City and on the Delaware Bay side of the county.||The high tides reached 7.40 feet above mean lower low at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 6.72 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County). Minor flooding was reported along Reeds Beach in Middle Township (Cape May County). Minor tidal flooding begins at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.

An Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped two to four inches of snow across southern New Jersey and an inch or less of snow across northern New Jersey on the 5th. Snow began during the late morning and ended during the evening on the 5th. While the snow was never heavy, most of the accumulating snow across southern New Jersey occurred during two spurts, one in the early afternoon and the second during the early evening. The highest accumulating snows occurred in a band across Salem, Cumberland, Gloucester and Atlantic Counties.||Specific accumulations included 4.4 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), 4.0 inches in Rio Grande (Cape May County) and North Vineland (Cumberland County), 3.8 inches in Pittsgrove (Salem County), 3.7 inches in Cape May City (Cape May County), 3.6 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County), 3.5 inches in Newport (Cumberland County) and Glassboro (Gloucester County), 3.2 inches in Hammonton (Atlantic County), 3.1 inches in Quinton (Salem County) and Absecon (Atlantic County), 3.0 inches in Atsion (Burlington County), 2.5 inches in Avalon (Cape May County) and Pennsville (Salem County), 2.0 inches in Manahawkin (Ocean County), 1.6 inches in Somerdale (Camden County) and 1.5 inches in Maple Shade (Burlington County).||As the name implies, the low pressure that caused the snow moved quickly from southern Alberta at 7 a.m. EST on the 4th to the central Ohio Valley at 7 a.m. EST on the 5th. By sunrise on the 6th, it was already well east of the Virginia coast.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region, which is known as cold air damming. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. This produced a strong onshore flow for a time on the 16th, which resulted in some areas of beach erosion and minor coastal flooding around Noon EST at the neap tides. Some roads were closed along the Monmouth County coast at the times of high tide. The storm system moved away from the area during the overnight hours of the 16th. No additional tidal flooding occurred with subsequent high tides.||In Monmouth County, minor beach erosion (1 to 3 foot vertical cuts) occurred in Sea Bright and from Ocean Grove south through Manasquan. In Ocean County, erosion was reported in Harvey Cedars, Surf City and Holgate. Harvey Cedars reported the worst erosion with vertical cuts reaching 3 to 4 feet. No beach erosion was reported in Atlantic County. In Cape May County, minor erosion (1 to 2 foot vertical cuts) were reported around Cape May City and on the Delaware Bay side of the county.||The high tides reached 7.40 feet above mean lower low at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 6.72 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County). Minor flooding was reported along Reeds Beach in Middle Township (Cape May County). Minor tidal flooding begins at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region, which is known as cold air damming. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. As a result, moisture was lifted up and over colder air trapped at the surface. This produced about 1 to 3 inches of snow and sleet across northern New Jersey, with up to a quarter of an inch of ice (from freezing rain). The storm system moved away from the area during the overnight hours of the 16th.||The combination of snow and mostly sleet made roadways rather slippery. In Union Township (Hunterdon County), 12 people were injured when a chartered bus carrying 31 passengers to New York City crashed, early in the morning on the 16th, into a jack-knifed tractor-trailer in the eastbound lanes of Interstate 78 near exit 15. The bus struck the truck, then crashed through the right guardrail and slid about 50 feet down a ravine. In Morris County, an icy ramp in Parsippany from eastbound Interstate 80 to eastbound Interstate 280, caused a series of vehicle accidents that closed the ramp at about 1 PM EST on the 16th. Numerous spin-outs and several accidents were reported across northern New Jersey. The ice build-up on trees and power lines resulted in power outages, which were reported in Phillipsburg (Warren County) and in 7 municipalities in Hunterdon County. Because of the ice, trees and wires were downed across parts of Hunterdon and Somerset Counties. There was an incident of a vehicle windshield being smashed out on Interstate 80 from ice flying off an SUV.||While there was some snow, sleet was the dominate precipitation type. Some total accumulations included 3.0 inches in Tewksbury (Hunterdon County); 2.5 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County); 2.1 inches in Wantage (Sussex County); 1.8 inches in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County), Blairstown (Warren County) and Sussex (Sussex County); 1.6 inches in Lake Parsippany (Morris County), Marcella (Morris County) and Bridgewater (Somerset County); 1.4 inches in Riegelsville (Warren County); 1.2 inches in Stewartsville (Warren County); and 1.0 inch in Flemington (Hunterdon County) and Whippany (Morris County). Ice accumulations from freezing rain included 0.25 of an inch in Tewksbury (Hunterdon County); 0.20 of an inch in Whitehouse (Hunterdon County) and Wantage (Sussex County); and 0.10 of an inch in Edison (Middlesex County).

A cold front moved through the region on December 12th. Behind this front, high pressure settled into northern New England providing colder air into the region. An area of low pressure then developed along the aforementioned front across the Tennessee Valley. This storm then tracked northeastward on December 13th, spreading moisture across the entire region. This storm produced one to as much as 6 inches of snow and sleet across northern New Jersey, however warmer air arrived well above the surface allowing freezing rain to fall for a time in parts of northern New Jersey. Some places picked up one-quarter of an inch of ice with more isolated areas getting as much as one-half inch of ice. The storm then moved out of the area during the first half of the night on the 13th.||The combination of snow, sleet and freezing rain lead to icy conditions across most of northern New Jersey. In Middlesex County, a Woodbridge firefighter was injured after their fire truck slid on the ice and hit two utility poles. Icing in Morris County caused trees to be downed onto train lines near Madison. Across northwestern New Jersey, schools and businesses closed early and that lead to an early start to the evening commute on the 13th. The State Police reported some 300 accidents occurred by late afternoon on the 13th.||Snow and sleet accumulations included 5.5 inches in Wantage (Sussex County); 5.0 inches in Sussex (Sussex County); 3.5 inches in Butler (Morris County), Marcella (Morris County) and Blairstown (Warren County); 3.0 inches in Boonton (Morris County) and Hopatcong (Sussex County); 2.0 inches in Belvidere (Warren County); 1.5 inches in Oakland Mills (Monmouth County); 0.8 of an inch in Edison (Middlesex County), Branchburg Park (Somerset County), Hamilton Square (Mercer County) and Hopewell (Mercer County); 0.6 of an inch in Stewartsville (Warren County); 0.5 of an inch in New Brunswick (Middlesex County), Hillsborough (Somerset County) and Whitehouse (Hunterdon County); 0.4 and inch in Bedminster (Somerset County) and 0.3 of an inch (all sleet) in Ewing (Mercer County). Ice accumulations due to freezing rain include, 0.50 of an inch in Chatham (Morris County); 0.30 in Blairstown (Warren County); 0.25 of an inch in Edison (Middlesex County) and 0.20 of an inch in New Brunswick (Middlesex County).

A wintry mix of precipitation fell across central and northwestern New Jersey from the early morning of the 2nd into the early morning of the 3rd. While precipitation changed to rain by the evening of the 2nd across much of the state, freezing rain persisted through the end of the event in Sussex County. Snow and sleet accumulations averaged one to three inches across the northern third of the state and an inch or less across the central third of the state. Ice accretions averaged one tenth to one-quarter of an inch with the highest amounts in Sussex County. Many traffic accidents were caused by this wintry mix.||Precipitation started as light snow from central New Jersey north during the early morning of the 2nd. South of Interstate 195 the snow ended around dawn. When precipitation moved back later that morning, it started as freezing rain and sleet. Because of the recent warm weather and indirect insolation from the sun, roads remained wet and the freezing rain was limited to exposed surfaces. Farther to the north in the state precipitation mixed with and then changed to freezing rain from the late morning into the afternoon.  As warmer air moved inland, the freezing rain changed to rain during the late afternoon in southern New Jersey, during the evening in central New Jersey and took to about Midnight EST on the 3rd for the change to plain rain to occur in most of northwest New Jersey. Precipitation remained freezing rain over most of Sussex County until the event ended around 3 a.m. EST on the 3rd.||In Ocean County, the light snow caused a seven vehicle pile-up near Stafford Township on the Garden State Parkway.  No serious injuries occurred, but the northbound lanes were closed for one hour. In Middlesex County, the freezing rain led to a fourteen vehicle pile-up on New Jersey State Route 18 between East Brunswick and Old Bridge. Four or five separate accidents merged into one.  No serious injuries were reported, but another accident caused an injury in Old Bridge. Farther northwest in New Jersey, several singular vehicle accidents especially on Interstate 80 occurred. The wintry weather forced the cancellation of the Christmas on the Farm Celebration in Wantage Township (Sussex County).| |Specific snow and sleet accumulations included 3.1 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 2.5 inches in Sussex and Wantage (Sussex County),  2.2 inches in Hackettstown (Warren County), 1.9 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County), 1.5 inches in Riegelsville (Hunterdon County) and Washington Crossing (Mercer County), 1.2 inches in Edison (Middlesex County), 1.0 inch in Colts Neck (Monmouth County) and Boonton (Morris County) and 0.5 inches in Mount Holly (Burlington County). ||The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that moved from the Great Lakes on the morning of the 2nd into the Saint Lawrence Valley on the morning of the 3rd. Even though the low pressure system moved north of the region, the cold air near the ground was difficult to dislodge. While it became warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, it still froze on contact especially over Sussex County.

A strong area of high pressure over far northern New England during the morning of December 21st, slowly moved eastward during the 22nd. Meanwhile, a storm system quickly moved off the Southeastern United States coast and out to sea. This setup allowed for a fairly prolonged period of onshore winds, which then turned to the south or southeast late on the 22nd or early on the 23rd. The combination of the persistent onshore flow, coupled with the full moon, produced areas of minor tidal flooding with the morning high tides on the 22nd and 23rd. The most widespread and highest tidal flooding occurred with the morning high tide on the 22nd. Highest tides included 7.07 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.78 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.

The combination of a steady rain and snow and ice melt on the 23rd helped produce stream and river flooding in parts of Somerset and Morris Counties later in the day on the 23rd into the early morning on the 25th. The heaviest rain fell between 3 pm EST and 9 pm EST that day. Storm totals averaged between 1 and 2 inches. Most of the stream and river flooding occurred in the northwest part of Somerset County and the northeast part of Morris County. The rain was caused by a strong low pressure system which moved from the upper Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 22nd, through the Great Lakes on the 23rd and into Quebec Province, Canada by the morning of the 24th. A strong high pressure system off the Canadian Maritimes on the 23rd assisted in pumping Atlantic Ocean moisture into this system.

A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region, which is known as cold air damming. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. This produced a strong onshore flow for a time on the 16th, which resulted in some areas of beach erosion and minor coastal flooding around Noon EST at the neap tides. Some roads were closed along the Monmouth County coast at the times of high tide. The storm system moved away from the area during the overnight hours of the 16th. No additional tidal flooding occurred with subsequent high tides.||In Monmouth County, minor beach erosion (1 to 3 foot vertical cuts) occurred in Sea Bright and from Ocean Grove south through Manasquan. In Ocean County, erosion was reported in Harvey Cedars, Surf City and Holgate. Harvey Cedars reported the worst erosion with vertical cuts reaching 3 to 4 feet. No beach erosion was reported in Atlantic County. A strong area of high pressure was anchored over southeastern Canada on December 15th. This high gradually moved eastward during the day, however it wedged itself down into the Mid Atlantic region. Meanwhile, a storm system became more organized across the Gulf Coast states. This storm tracked northeastward from the 15th to the 16th. As the main storm tracked into Ohio on the 16th, a secondary area of low pressure developed near southern Delaware during that morning. Strong winds resulted as the storm intensified, mostly as the storm itself began to depart during the evening and overnight on the 16th. The combination of a steady rain and snow and ice melt on the 23rd helped produce stream and river flooding in parts of Somerset and Morris Counties later in the day on the 23rd into the early morning on the 25th. The heaviest rain fell between 3 pm EST and 9 pm EST that day. Storm totals averaged between 1 and 2 inches. Most of the stream and river flooding occurred in the northwest part of Somerset County and the northeast part of Morris County. The rain was caused by a strong low pressure system which moved from the upper Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 22nd, through the Great Lakes on the 23rd and into Quebec Province, Canada by the morning of the 24th. A strong high pressure system off the Canadian Maritimes on the 23rd assisted in pumping Atlantic Ocean moisture into this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Pompton River at Midwood Road in Lincoln Park was above its 8.5 foot flood stage from 3 a.m. EST through 840 a.m. EST on the 24th. It crested at 8.7 feet at 8 a.m. EST. The Rockaway River below the Boonton Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 1022 a.m. EST on the 24th through 153 a.m. EST on the 25th. It crested at 5.23 feet at 330 p.m. EST on the 24th. The East Ditch at Beaver Brook was above its 3.9 foot flood stage from 1030 p.m. EST on the 23rd through 659 a.m. EST on the 24th. It crested at 4.2 feet at 1 a.m. EST on the 24th.||Specific storm totals included 2.20 inches in Boonton below the reservoir, 1.40 inches in Lincoln Park and 1.36 inches in Pequannock.

A strong area of high pressure over far northern New England during the morning of December 21st, slowly moved eastward during the 22nd. Meanwhile, a storm system quickly moved off the Southeastern United States coast and out to sea. This setup allowed for a fairly prolonged period of onshore winds, which then turned to the south or southeast late on the 22nd or early on the 23rd. The combination of the persistent onshore flow, coupled with the full moon, produced areas of minor tidal flooding with the morning high tides on the 22nd and 23rd. The most widespread and highest tidal flooding occurred with the morning high tide on the 22nd. Highest tides included 7.07 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.78 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. Moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.

An approaching cold front helped trigger light snow across northwest New Jersey during the afternoon and early evening of the 7th. Accumulations averaged only one to two inches, but with temperatures below freezing, untreated roadways were slippery and slowed the afternoon commute.

A lake effect snow band caused moderate to heavy snow showers to occur across about a ten mile wide band centered across northern Warren County, southern Morris County and northeast Somerset County during the morning of the 5th. A weaker band also occurred in Sussex County. Within this band, accumulations reached around two inches. Strong winds accompanied the snow showers and caused very poor visibilities and hazardous driving conditions. The heaviest snow fell from Blairstown in Warren County eastsoutheast across Independence and Hackettstown in Warren County,  Washington, Chester, Mendham and Long Hill Townships in Morris County and Bernards and Bernardsville in Somerset County.||Dozens of minor accidents occurred including a collision between a school bus and a pickup truck in Morris County. In Chester Township (Morris County), a pickup truck slid past a stop sign and struck the side of a school bus. Two children suffered minor head injuries.  In Mendham Township, about 30 vehicles skidded off roads. In Chatham Township, a four vehicle pileup occurred on Southern Boulevard. Other accidents in Morris County were reported Randolph Township.||Specific accumulations included 3.0 inches in Mount Olive (Morris County), 2.0 inches in Succasunna (Morris County) and Blairstown (Warren County), 1.0 inch in Hope (Warren County) and Chatham (Morris County), 0.8 inches in Wantage (Sussex County) and 0.5 inches in Hillsborough (Somerset County).

Strong northwest to west winds occurred during the daytime hours on January 20th across New Jersey. It was caused by an intense pressure gradient (large difference in surface pressure over a relatively small area) between a powerful low pressure system (960 millibars at 7 a.m. EST on the 20th) moving through the Saint Lawrence Valley and a high pressure system building east from the Midwest. Peak wind gusts averaged 45 to 50 mph at the coast and around 45 mph inland. Peak wind gusts included 50 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 49 mph at Barnegat Light (Ocean County), 48 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 47 mph at the nearby Philadelphia International Airport in Pennsylvania, 45 mph at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County) and Cape May (Cape May County), 44 mph in Millville (Cumberland County) and 43 mph at the Newark International Airport in Essex County.

Snow squalls and heavy snow showers developed along a southward moving arctic cold front in southern New Jersey during the evening of the 25th. Within this band of heavier snow, accumulations averaged 1 to 3 inches across southern Gloucester County, northern Salem, Cumberland and Cape May Counties and in Atlantic County. Untreated roadways were hazardous. Luckily the heavy snow fell after the evening commute was done. Specific accumulations included 3.0 inches in Egg Harbor Township (Atlantic County), 2.5 inches in Glassboro (Gloucester County) and Mays Landing (Atlantic County), 2.3 inches in Margate (Atlantic County), 1.7 inches in Pittsgrove (Salem County), 1.5 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 1.4 inches in Ocean City (Cape May County) and 1.2 inches in Estell Manor (Atlantic County).

A low pressure system that passed well to the south of New Jersey helped produce light snow across central New Jersey and a wintry mix of precipitation across southern New Jersey to the south of Burlington County. Snowfall accumulations were generally an inch or less and ice accretions were just a couple of hundredths of an inch. Untreated roads were very hazardous as air temperatures were below freezing throughout the event. The only reported fatal accident occurred in a location where precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling around 6 p.m. EST on the 21st. South of Burlington County, as warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed to freezing rain and sleet after midnight EST on the 22nd. Precipitation ended around 5 a.m. EST on the 22nd.  |In Burlington County, in New Hanover Township, a 21-year-old male died. He lost control of his vehicle on the northbound lane of Cookstown-Browns Mills Road, drove off the road and crashed into a wood fence.  Actual snow accumulations included 2.2 inches in Cape May (Cape May County), 1.0 inch in West Deptford (Gloucester County), 0.8 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County) and Toms River (Ocean County), 0.7 inches in Wrightstown (Burlington County), 0.5 inches in Absecon (Atlantic County), the Atlantic City International Airport, Seabrook (Cumberland County) and Winslow (Camden County) and 0.4 inches in Pittsgrove (Salem County). |The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed along the western Gulf Coast on the 20th. The low pressure system moved northeast (around Noon EST on the 21st, it was located along the Alabama and Mississippi border) and a secondary low pressure system formed on its warm front over eastern North Carolina overnight on the 21st. The secondary low pressure system became the primary low and moved quickly eastnortheast off of the North Carolina coast during the morning of the 22nd. Another low pressure system moved through the Ohio Valley on the 21st before it weakened and dissipated that night. This low though was able to pump in warmer air aloft and help change the precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The New Year began on a very wet note across New Jersey as a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico helped drop heavy rain across the state. Rain began falling at or slightly after the New Year began and ended during the early afternoon. The heaviest rain fell in the west central and southwest part of the state. This caused poor drainage flooding, contributed to minor tidal flooding along tidal sections of the Delaware River and some river flooding in Somerset County. Storm totals across the state averaged 1 to 2 inches with the lightest amounts in Warren and Sussex Counties.|The slow moving frontal system that wrung the heavy rain across the region moved from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of December 31st, east to the Ohio Valley and southern Appalachians at 7 p.m. EST on the 31st, to the central Appalachians at 1 a.m. EST on January 1st. As the front started to cross the Pennsylvania, Maryland and West Virginia Appalachians during the morning of January 1st, a low pressure system formed on the triple point of the occluded front in northeastern Virginia around 7 a.m. EST on January 1st. The low pressure system then moved northeast through the Delmarva Peninsula and into New Jersey by the early afternoon of January 1st. At 7 p.m. EST on January 1st, the low pressure system reached western Long Island.

The combination of a low pressure system emerging from the Gulf of Mexico that merged with a cold front approaching from the center of the United States caused heavy rain to fall from the evening of the 7th into the early morning of the 8th. As the warm front cleared New Jersey, the heavier rain ended. The rain ended as the front cleared the nearby coastal waters.

Storm totals included 2.19 inches in Somerdale, 1.60 inches in Pennsauken and 1.50 inches in Blue Anchor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The New Year began on a very wet note across New Jersey as a deep southerly flow from the Gulf of Mexico helped drop heavy rain across the state. Rain began falling at or slightly after the New Year began and ended during the early afternoon. The heaviest rain fell in the west central and southwest part of the state. This caused poor drainage flooding, contributed to minor tidal flooding along tidal sections of the Delaware River and some river flooding in Somerset County. Storm totals across the state averaged 1 to 2 inches with the lightest amounts in Warren and Sussex Counties.|The slow moving frontal system that wrung the heavy rain across the region moved from the Tennessee Valley on the morning of December 31st, east to the Ohio Valley and southern Appalachians at 7 p.m. EST on the 31st, to the central Appalachians at 1 a.m. EST on January 1st. As the front started to cross the Pennsylvania, Maryland and West Virginia Appalachians during the morning of January 1st, a low pressure system formed on the triple point of the occluded front in northeastern Virginia around 7 a.m. EST on January 1st. The low pressure system then moved northeast through the Delmarva Peninsula and into New Jersey by the early afternoon of January 1st. At 7 p.m. EST on January 1st, the low pressure system reached western Long Island.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain storm totals from Morris County included 1.80 inches in Beaver Brook, 1.76 inches in Boonton, 1.68 inches in Morris Plains, 1.60 inches in Morristown and 1.52 inches in Pequannock. While gauged rivers in the Passaic River Basin stayed within bankfull, the heavy rain in the county contributed to flooding along the North Branch of the Raritan River in Somerset County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system that passed well to the south of New Jersey helped produce light snow across central New Jersey and a wintry mix of precipitation across southern New Jersey to the south of Burlington County. Snowfall accumulations were generally an inch or less and ice accretions were just a couple of hundredths of an inch. Untreated roads were very hazardous as air temperatures were below freezing throughout the event. The only reported fatal accident occurred in a location where precipitation fell as snow. Snow began falling around 6 p.m. EST on the 21st. South of Burlington County, as warmer air moved in aloft, the snow changed to freezing rain and sleet after midnight EST on the 22nd. Precipitation ended around 5 a.m. EST on the 22nd.  |In Burlington County, in New Hanover Township, a 21-year-old male died. He lost control of his vehicle on the northbound lane of Cookstown-Browns Mills Road, drove off the road and crashed into a wood fence.  Actual snow accumulations included 2.2 inches in Cape May (Cape May County), 1.0 inch in West Deptford (Gloucester County), 0.8 inches in New Lisbon (Burlington County) and Toms River (Ocean County), 0.7 inches in Wrightstown (Burlington County), 0.5 inches in Absecon (Atlantic County), the Atlantic City International Airport, Seabrook (Cumberland County) and Winslow (Camden County) and 0.4 inches in Pittsgrove (Salem County). |The precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed along the western Gulf Coast on the 20th. The low pressure system moved northeast (around Noon EST on the 21st, it was located along the Alabama and Mississippi border) and a secondary low pressure system formed on its warm front over eastern North Carolina overnight on the 21st. The secondary low pressure system became the primary low and moved quickly eastnortheast off of the North Carolina coast during the morning of the 22nd. Another low pressure system moved through the Ohio Valley on the 21st before it weakened and dissipated that night. This low though was able to pump in warmer air aloft and help change the precipitation over to sleet and freezing rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain associated with the passage of a strong cold front in combination with a melting snow pack caused the Ho-Ho-Kus River at Ho-Ho-Kus to overflow its' banks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Ho-Ho-Kus River at Ho-Ho-Kus exceeded its' flood stage of 3.0 feet on Dec. 23, 2007 at 9:30 pm with a stage of 3.03 feet. The river crested at 3.31 feet on Dec. 23, 2007 at 11:15 pm and returned within its' banks on Dec. 24, 2007 at 1:45 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the nor'easter intensified as it headed into the New England coastal waters and the Gulf of Maine, strong northwest winds enveloped New Jersey during the late afternoon and the evening of the 14th. Peak wind gusts averaged between 40 and 55 mph and hampered power restoration efforts where significant accretions of ice occurred across central New Jersey. The highest winds occurred along the coast and the higher terrain in the northwest part of the state. |Peak wind gusts included 57 mph in Wantage (Sussex County), 56 mph in Cape May Harbor (Cape May), 49 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County), 48 mph in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County), 47 mph in Keansburg (Monmouth County) and Seaside Heights (Ocean County), 46 mph in Atlantic City (Atlantic County) and 40 mph in Trenton.

A fast moving Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped 1.0 to 3.0 inches of snow across Cumberland and Cape May Counties in New Jersey during the morning of the 7th. Accumulations across the rest of southern New Jersey were an inch or less and in northern New Jersey were less than half an inch. Snow began falling between 1 a.m. and 2 a.m. EST on the 7th and ended around 8 a.m. EST that morning. Temperatures were below freezing as the snow fell and it quickly accumulated on all surfaces. Untreated roadways and walkways were all slippery. Specific accumulations included 3.0 inches in Leesburg and Newport (Cumberland County), 2.5 inches in Dias Creek (Cape May County), 2.4 inches in West Cape May (Cape May County), 2.0 inches in Green Creek (Cape May County), 1.5 inches in Cape May (Cape May County), 1.0 in West Deptford (Gloucester County), 0.8 inches in Pittsgrove (Salem County) and Lindenwold (Camden County) and 0.7 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport. The Alberta Clipper responsible for the snow raced from South Dakota around sunrise on the 6th to just east of the southern Delmarva Peninsula on the morning of the 7th. The low pressure system was moving too fast to drop heavier snow across the region.

A fast moving Alberta Clipper low pressure system dropped 1.0 to 3.0 inches of snow across Cumberland and Cape May Counties in New Jersey during the morning of the 7th. Accumulations across the rest of southern New Jersey were an inch or less and in northern New Jersey were less than half an inch. Snow began falling between 1 a.m. and 2 a.m. EST on the 7th and ended around 8 a.m. EST that morning. Temperatures were below freezing as the snow fell and it quickly accumulated on all surfaces. Untreated roadways and walkways were all slippery. Specific accumulations included 3.0 inches in Leesburg and Newport (Cumberland County), 2.5 inches in Dias Creek (Cape May County), 2.4 inches in West Cape May (Cape May County), 2.0 inches in Green Creek (Cape May County), 1.5 inches in Cape May (Cape May County), 1.0 in West Deptford (Gloucester County), 0.8 inches in Pittsgrove (Salem County) and Lindenwold (Camden County) and 0.7 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport. The Alberta Clipper responsible for the snow raced from South Dakota around sunrise on the 6th to just east of the southern Delmarva Peninsula on the morning of the 7th. The low pressure system was moving too fast to drop heavier snow across the region.

A renewed strong northwest flow coupled with the astronomical spring (low) tides around the new moon caused blow-out tides around the times of the morning low tide on the 19th. The low tide In Philadelphia reached 2.53 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water. Unlike previous blow-out tides this month, the northwest flow diminished quickly and was only a one low tide cycle event.

Another strong northwest flow between a high pressure system building in from Canada and a low pressure system deepening over the Canadian Maritimes brought another unseasonably cold shot of air into northwestern New Jersey. Morning wind chills on the 19th were between zero and 10 degrees below zero. Actual low temperatures were in the positive single numbers. Since this occurred on President's Day, it did not have the same effect as a typical weekday day. Low temperatures included zero at High Point (Sussex County), 5 degrees in Randolph (Morris County), 6 degrees in Hope (Warren County), 7 degrees in Andover (Sussex County), 8 degrees in Parsippany (Morris County) and Pequest (Somerset County) and 9 degrees in Basking Ridge (Somerset County), Phillipsburg (Warren County) and Wertsville (Hunterdon County).

A persistent northwest to west flow following the winter storm on February 13th and 14th culminated in blow-out tides around the times of low tide during the late afternoon and early evening of the 16th and the morning of the 17th. The lowest tides occurred on the morning of the 17th. The new moon assisted, because spring tides cause the biggest differences in water levels between high and low tides. Low tide in Philadelphia (Pennsylvania) reached 2.3 feet below mean lower low water. Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water. The west flow subsided later in the day on the 17th.

In the wake of the February 13th and 14th nor'easter, unseasonably cold air was drawn southward into New Jersey on gusty west to northwest winds on the 15th and 16th. Across northwest New Jersey, morning lows were in the positive single numbers both days and wind chill factors reached as low as 10 degrees below zero. Actual low temperatures included 2 degrees at High Point (Sussex County), 5 degrees in Walpack and Wantage (Sussex County), 7 degrees in Randolph (Morris County) and Hope (Warren County), and 8 degrees in Hackettstown (Warren County) and Sussex (Sussex County).

A wintry mix of light freezing rain and sleet fell during the early morning of the 2nd across interior southeast New Jersey. Precipitation moved in shortly after midnight EST on the 2nd. Temperatures slowly rose overnight and by 7 a.m. EST in the morning, it was warm enough for precipitation to fall as plain rain. Untreated roadways and walkways were slippery. Ice accretions were generally less than one tenth of an inch. The wintry mix of precipitation was caused by a low pressure system that developed over the southeastern United States on the 1st and moved northeast and passed well east of New Jersey on the morning of the 2nd. New Jersey was located along the northwestern edge of the precipitation shield.

A strong cold front moved through New Jersey during the afternoon of the 22nd. The northwest flow between a deepening low pressure at the head of the front and a high pressure system building southeast from Canada intensified during the night of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. The strongest wind gusts occurred in southeastern New Jersey with lower winds elsewhere in the state. In the southeast part of the state peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 48 mph in the Cape May Harbor (Cape May County), 45 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, 43 mph at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County) and 41 mph in Millville (Cumberland County).

A strong cold front moved through New Jersey during the afternoon of the 22nd. The northwest flow between a deepening low pressure at the head of the front and a high pressure system building southeast from Canada intensified during the night of the 22nd into the morning of the 23rd. The strongest wind gusts occurred in southeastern New Jersey with lower winds elsewhere in the state. In the southeast part of the state peak wind gusts averaged around 45 mph and included 48 mph in the Cape May Harbor (Cape May County), 45 mph at the Atlantic City International Airport, 43 mph at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County) and 41 mph in Millville (Cumberland County).

An arctic air mass that originated near the North Pole invaded New Jersey. The combination of the unseasonably cold air and gusty northwest winds produced wind chill factors as low as zero to around 10 degrees below zero during the mornings of the 5th and 6th. The extreme cold caused the death of two people. A 50-year-old male in Camden (Camden County) died in a field on the 6th a he walked home from a relative's house. An 84-year-old woman was found dead next to her car and walker in her driveway in Washington Township (Warren County).  |The extrteme cold caused many municipalities to declare Code Blue winter weather alerts. This sets into motion efforts by churches, local charitable organizations and outreach programs to open shelters and help assist getting the homeless indoors. Some Rescue Mission shelters in Mercer County were filled to twice of capacity. Many schools in northwestern New Jersey had delayed openings both mornings. Many pipes froze across the region and many vehicles had dead batteries. The AAA responded to nearly 1,000 service calls, half of them battery related.  Firefighters had a particular difficult time battling blazes in these conditions as in some instances water froze in tanker trucks and fire hydrants froze. This was evident as fire fighters battled a particularly stubborn blaze in a condominium development in Sea Bright (Monmouth County). Two firefighters and a 57-year-old womean were injured. The firefighters and trucks were covered in ice. The extreme cold helped the South Jersey Gas Company set an new daily consumption record of 432,594 decatherms. As a ridge of high pressure extending from north of the Great Lakes southeast across southern New England moved slowly northeast, a low pressure system developed off the Carolina coast.  As this low moved north to northeast just off the mid Atlantic coast, it rapidly intensified during Wednesday morning.  This low moved across eastern Long Island during the early afternoon hours. |These systems produced snow, sleet, freezing rain, rain, strong gusty winds, and minor tidal flooding across the region. |Light snow developed from southwest to northeast during Tuesday evening between 5 p.m. and 10 p.m.  It mixed with sleet and freezing rain, first across eastern Long Island between midnight and 3 a.m. and then across the remainder of the area between 3 a.m. and 6 a.m.  By 6 a.m., a mixed bag of sleet and freezing rain occurred everywhere with temperatures around 20 degrees across Northeast New Jersey and metro New York. |As the deepening low moved more to the northeast, cold air was locked in west of the storm track.  Six hour precipitation totals between 7 a.m. and 1 p.m. were between 0.50 and 0.75 inches.  This fell as mainly plain rain across eastern Long Island and coastal Connecticut and mainly sleet with some freezing rain elsewhere.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mixture of heavy snow, sleet, and freezing rain fell across the county from late Tuesday evening, Feb. 13, through Wednesday afternoon, Feb. 14. Snow and ice totals ranged from 5 to 7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A grass fire in Egg Harbor Township closed the northbound and southbound lanes of the Garden State Parkway near the intersection with the Atlantic City Expressway at 230 p.m. EST. Heavy smoke sharply reduced visibility. Later that afternoon, one lane in each direction on the Garden State Parkway was reopened. Traffic was also diverted on the Atlantic City Expressway. The spread of the brush fire was assisted by the gusty northwest winds. The peak wind gust at the Atlantic City International Airport on the 5th was 46 mph.

A persistent northwest flow started on February 4th and continued into February 6th. This caused blowout tides in Delaware Bay and along the Ocean on the 4th and 5th during the times of low tides. The lowest tides occurred with the early morning low tide on the 5th. Coming off of the full moon assisted, because spring tides cause the biggest differences in water levels between high and low tides. Low tides reached 2.4 feet below mean lower low water in Atlantic City (Atlantic County), 2.3 feet below mean lower low water at the Cape May Ferry Terminal (Cape May County) and 1.9 feet below mean lower low water at Cape May Harbor (Cape May County). Blowout tides start at 1.8 feet below mean lower low water. The northwest flow subsided on February 6th and the tide cycle was also further removed from spring tides.

A combination of snow, ice and wind brought down trees and power lines, which cut the power to roughly 80,000 customers in Monmouth, northern Ocean, Middlesex and Burlington counties by the early afternoon of the 14th. At 1:30 PM on the 14th, state troopers had responded to more than 1,250 accidents on New Jersey roads since 5 PM on the 13th. The state police responded to more than 900 accidents between 5 AM and 1 PM on the 14th. There were three fatalities, including two in which motorists left their vehicles after minor accidents and then were struck by another vehicle. On the 14th, the speed limit on the New Jersey Turnpike was lowered to 35 mph due to the slippery conditions along with reduced visibilities. New Jersey Transit reported no serious problems or major delays on its commuter-rail lines and only minor delays on its bus routes. Public Service Electric and Gas reported nearly 4,300 outages at 11:30 AM on the 14th. At least 7,000 Atlantic City Electric customers were without power in South Jersey by the early afternoon of the 14th, most in Salem and Gloucester counties. In total, an estimated 55,000 Atlantic City Electric customers lost power as a result of the storm. Jersey Central Power and Light had more than 100,000 customers without power during the height of the storm.|An area of low pressure tracked across southeastern Canada during the 12th, which brought an arctic front across the Mid Atlantic region late on the 12th. As this occurred, a strong area of high pressure located across central Canada built down into the Northeast, allowing very cold air to spill southward ultimately leading to what is known as cold air damming. This occurs east of the mountains down through the Mid Atlantic states. Meanwhile, a Pacific storm slammed onto the California coast during the 11th and as it moved eastward, a new area of low pressure developed across southeastern Colorado and the Texas Panhandle. This storm became impressive as it moved into the Ohio Valley during the 13th. However as additional energy moved into the eastern part of the country, a new area of low pressure developed near the North Carolina coast late on the 13th. This allowed mainly light snow to overspread the region from south to north during the early morning hours of the 13th. As the secondary area of low pressure became more organized to our south, moisture began to fill in across North Carolina and Virginia and this moved northward. The arctic front that slipped through the state on the 12th was positioned across the southern Delmarva and this began a northward jog during the night of the 13th and the morning of the 14th. The intensifying area of low pressure tracked very near southern Delaware and then slid close to the New Jersey Shore during the morning of the 14th. This system continued to intensify as it moved into New England during the afternoon and evening of the 14th. The track of the storm kept winds at the surface from the northeast, which pretty much locked in the cold air at the surface for the inland areas while some warmer air worked in above the surface. The upper-level system then passed overhead during the evening of the 14th, with the entire system pretty much a memory by late in the evening of the 14th. |Snow began across New Jersey around 7 AM EST on the 13th from Camden to Atlantic Counties on southward; between 8 AM and 10 AM EST across Burlington and Ocean Counties; between 12 PM and 3 PM EST in Warren, Hunterdon, Mercer and Monmouth Counties, and around 5 PM EST in Sussex, Morris, Somerset and Middlesex Counties. There was a general lull in the precipitation during the evening hours of the 13th especially across the central and southern areas, however the precipitation became widespread overnight and during the early morning hours of the 14th. As some warmer air began to move in above the surface, the snow mixed with and changed to sleet and freezing rain inland between 6 PM and 7:15 PM EST on the 13th from Burlington and Ocean Counties on southward. Elsewhere, the changeover occurred generally between 7:45 PM and 11 PM EST on the 13th for Mercer, Middlesex and Monmouth Counties, and for the counties farther to the north the changeover was delayed until the early morning hours of the 14th.  Milder air made its way to the Shore and about 20 miles inland to change the mixed precipitation over to plain rain around 2 AM EST on the 14th. There was a fairly tight gradient in temperatures and precipitation types as icing continued for an extended period of time for the inland communities. Areas closest to the Delaware River saw mostly sleet once the changeover from snow occurred, while areas farther eastward saw more freezing rain. This produced significant icing in many areas of interior New Jersey, causing the downing of some trees and power lines. Ice accumulated to a half of an inch thick in Tinton Falls (Monmouth County), which downed numerous trees. At least 7,000 Atlantic City Electric customers were without power in South Jersey by the early afternoon of the 14th, most in Salem and Gloucester counties.
