At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.

Heavy rain that fell during the first half of the day on the 12th caused poor drainage flooding in Monmouth County and flooding along parts of the Millstone River in Somerset County. It also left the area vulnerable for the heavier rain that was to follow on the 15th. The low pressure system that caused the heavy rain moved from the Mississippi Valley on the morning of the 11th through the Great Lakes on the 12th. The heavy rain ended just as its occluded front moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The runoff from the 1 to 2 inches of rain that fell in the Millstone River Basin caused minor flooding of the Millstone River around Griggstown. The Millstone River at Griggstown was above its 10 foot flood stage from 518 p.m. EDT on the 12th through 1015 a.m. EDT on the 13th. It crested at 10.35 feet at 115 a.m. EDT on the 13th.

At one time over 70 major roadways and interstates were closed including New Jersey State Routes 20, 23, 38, 46, 70 and 73 as well as U.S. Route 30 and Interstates 80 and 380. There were over 700 traffic accidents indirectly caused by the flooding and heavy rain. Three people in the state drowned. The strong winds caused about 120,000 homes and businesses in the state to lose power. Power was not completely restored until the 18th. Even Rutgers University was closed on the 16th and 17th. In the Raritan Basin, school closings lasted into the week of April 23rd. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours in affected areas. The heavy and flooding rains was expected to delay the spring planting on farms. Garden center and nursery business which was already slow because of the unseasonably cold weather was dealt another set-back. EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Somerset County, the flooding was the worst since the remnants of Hurricane Floyd in September of 1999. In a similar scene to Floyd in 1999, three homes were destroyed on East Street as firefighters could not make their way through flood waters to extinguish the blaze. It crested at 8.95 feet at 630 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The Lamington River at Burnt Mills was above its 9.5 foot flood stage from 1150 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 715 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 12.1 at 1100 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The Green Brook at Plainfield was above its 8 foot flood stage from 354 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 747 p.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 9.52 feet at 630 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The Passaic River at Millington was above its 8 foot flood stage from 848 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 235 a.m. EDT on the 19th. In Medford Lakes a dam under repair breached near Beach Drive between Upper and Lower Aetna Lakes. In Edgewater Park, twenty-six homes were evacuated in the Silver Park West Senior Citizen Community after a retention basin overflowed. In Eastampton Township, the Ewansville section near the North Branch of the Rancocas Creek was evacuated. Flooding farther downstream affected the municipal building in Mount Holly and flooded the parking lots of the county buildings. Flooding along the South Branch of the Rancocas Creek affected the Vincentown section of Southampton Township. In Camden County, the Carolina Lake Dam breached between Waterford and Medford. This flooded the Mullica River and forced the closure of Jackson Road in Medford. Farther downstream, it caused Atsion Lake to overflow and forced the closure of U.S. Route 206 in Shamong Township on the 17th. Sylvan Lake overflowed in Burlington Township. |The North Branch of the Rancocas Creek at Pemberton was above its 2.5 foot flood stage from 745 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 145 p.m. EDT on the 20th. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain and stream and river flooding forced about 1000 people to be evacuated throughout Morris County. About half of those evacuated still could not return to their home as of April 26th. Evacuations occurred in Lincoln Park, Pequannock, Parsippany, Denville, Hanover and East Hanover. In addition, about 100 businesses along New Jersey State Route 23 suffered flood damage.|In Lincoln Park, flooding along the Pompton River severely affected the borough. About 1000 homes were damaged with water up to five feet in some homes. Twenty-four roads were closed. There was no access to New Jersey State Routes 23 and Interstate 80. Two Bridges Road was expected to be closed into May. In Pequannock Township, about 100 residents were evacuated near the Pompton River. About 400 homes were damaged. New Jersey State Route 23 was closed. In Parsippany Township, the Partridge Run Apartments on New Road near the Rockaway River was evacuated. The Lake Hiawatha neighborhood was also evacuated. About 450 homes and apartments were flooded, with 120 needing repairs. In Hanover Township, a few roads were closed including New Jersey State Route 10. A landslide occurred behind an office complex. The buildings were not damaged. The Whippany River flooded. Whippany River flooding also forced flood waters into the backyards and basements in Morristown. In Netcong, several residents and businesses were evacuated around the Railroad Avenue Way. In Denville Township, flooding along the Den Brook and Rockaway River closed many roads. In Montville Township, roads and properties were flooded in the Pine Brook area along the Passaic River. In Randolph Township, Shongum Lake overflowed.|The Rockaway River at Boonton Above the Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 745 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 837 a.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 6.69 feet at 1115 a.m. EDT on the 16th. This was the 3rd highest crest on record and the highest since January 25th, 1979. The Rockaway River at Boonton Below the Reservoir was above its 5 foot flood stage from 809 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 401 p.m. EDT on the 19th. It crested at 8.09 feet at 1230 p.m. EDT on the 16th. This was the 4th highest crest on record and the highest since April 6th, 1984. The Passaic River at Chatham was above its 6 foot flood stage from 219 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 445 a.m. EDT on the 21st. It crested at 7.45 feet at 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th. Farther downstream, the Passaic River at Pine Brook was above its 19 foot flood stage from 958 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 125 p.m. EDT on he 23rd. It crested at 22.32 feet at 131 a.m. EDT on the 18th. It was the 3rd highest crest on record and the highest since April 7, 1984. Farther downstream, the Passaic River at Two Bridges was above its 9 foot flood stage from 1012 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 615 p.m. EDT on the 23rd. It crested at 15.01 feet at 1200 a.m. on the 18th. It is now the highest crest on record. The Pequannock River at Macopin Dam was above its 5.5 foot flood stage from 625 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 815 a.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 6.38 feet at 1200 p.m. EDT on the 16th. The Pompton River at Pompton Plains was above its 16 foot flood stage from 843 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 112 p.m. EDT on the 18th. It crested at 21.72 feet at 600 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It was the highest crest since April 6, 1984. Farther downstream, the Pompton River at Midwood was above its 8.5 foot flood stage at 500 p.m. EDT on the 15th. It crested at 15.5 feet at 100 p.m. EDT on the 16th. The gage became stuck and did not record when the river went below flood stage. The Whippany River at Morristown was above its 6 foot flood stage from 312 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 1033 a.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 6.79 feet at 1130 p.m. EDT on the 15th. The East Ditch at Beaver Brook was above its 3.9 foot flood stage from 340 p.m. EDT on the 15th through 229 a.m. EDT on the 17th. It crested at 5.3 feet at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th.|Precipitation totals included 5.84 inches in Parsippany, 5.22 inches in Charlotteburg, 4.82 inches in Randolph, 4.60 inches in Oak Ridge, 4.49 inches in Boonton, and 4.14 inches in Netcong.|

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A run of unseasonably cold weather produce below freezing minimum temperatures from the morning of the 5th through the morning of the 11th. The coldest morning for most places was the morning of the 11th when the winds were the lightest. This caused minor damage to early blooming fruit trees such as apricots and peaches. The unseasonably cold weather also slowed the germination and planting of early crops. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A wildfire charred about 100 acres of brush at a Sunoco Refinery in West Deptford Township (Gloucester County) on the afternoon of the 6th. The fire began about 130 p.m. EDT in a wooded area and the wind pushed it into a field. It raged for nearly three hours before about one hundered firefighters from ten different companies were able to control it. The recent dry weather and strong winds during the afternoon carried flames across dry patches of fields between the refinery and homes along Red Bank Avenue. No homes on Red Bank Avenue or Ninth Street were evacuated. Flames came within 100 feet of homes on Ninth Street before they were stopped. The peak wind gust at Philadelphia International Airport that afternoon was 30 mph from the east.

Highest wind gusts averaged between 45 and 50 mph and included 52 mph at the Atlantic City Marina (Atlantic County) and 46 mph in Barnegat (Ocean County) and Cape May Harbor (Cape May County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down numerous trees and wires in Morris County and caused the greatest damage in the northeast part of the county. In Butler Borough, a downed tree damaged one vehicle. Several trees were knocked down throughout the borough. A downed wire started a small fire on Tintle Road. In Pequannock Township, downed trees were reported on several side streets. About 8,000 homes and businesses lost power in Morris County. In addition to Butler and Pequannock, downed wires were also reported in Mendham Township, Morristown, Mount Olive Township and Randolph Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front that coincided with daytime heating produced a line of severe thunderstorms across New Jersey during the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires mainly in the southwestern part of the county. In East Brunswick Township, a downed tree damaged the roof of one home. Downed wires closed two roads. The northbound side of U.S. Route 1 was reduced to one lane because of downed wires. In South Brunswick Township, trees were knocked down on Broadway Road. A tree was also knocked down in Woodbridge Township. The thunderstorms caused the postponement of a couple of activities: the reception for the Edison Memorial Tower and Museum and the Greater Middlesex Conference Girls lacrosse tournament championship.

An intense area of low pressure on the 1st moved into the Upper Midwest with a cold front stretched southward across the Ohio Valley all the way to the Gulf of Mexico. Meanwhile, a cold front moved through the Northeast and settled into the Mid Atlantic region. This boundary remained stationary for awhile during the overnight hours of the 1st, as the strong low pressure system moved into the western Great Lakes. As cold air at the surface lingered to the north of the aforementioned boundary, precipitation moved in and caused a period of freezing rain. Gradually an occluded front moved through the area during the morning hours of the 2nd, as a weak area of low pressure developed over eastern Pennsylvania. This allowed warmer air to move in and the precipitation to taper off.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cold air locked in at the surface and as precipitation started at 11:00 PM EST on the 1st, freezing rain occurred. The icing was mostly confined to the higher elevations where temperatures remain at or just below freezing for several hours. During the early morning hours of the 2nd, a trained weather spotter in Wantage reported that ice accumulations ranged from a coating up to a quarter of an inch on some surfaces. The freezing rain changed to plain rain around 6:00 AM EST on the 2nd.

An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to New Jersey that started on the 15th. The worst flooding occurred along the Raritan and Passaic River Basins. It was the worst flooding in the Raritan Basin since Hurricane Floyd in 1999. Bound Brook and Manville were once again hit hard as were communities on the other side of the Raritan River in Middlesex County. Along the Passaic Basin, Lincoln Park also was hit hard. The nor'easter also brought strong to high winds as well as some snow to the state on the 16th. Numerous streams and rivers flooded, but the flooding along the Delaware River was minimal.  |Statewide damage was estimated at $180 million dollars. It was the second worst rain storm (not related to a hurricane) in the state's history. Acting Governor Richard J. Codey declared a state of emergency on the 16th. About 5,000 people were evacuated in 11 of the state's 21 counties. At one time over 70 major roadways and interstates were closed including New Jersey State Routes 20, 23, 38, 46, 70 and 73 as well as U.S. Route 30 and Interstates 80 and 380. There were over 700 traffic accidents indirectly caused by the flooding and heavy rain. Three people in the state drowned. The strong winds caused about 120,000 homes and businesses in the state to lose power. Power was not completely restored until the 18th. The combination of the snow, wind (power outages) and flooding caused many school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings on the 16th. Even Rutgers University was closed on the 16th and 17th. In the Raritan Basin, school closings lasted into the week of April 23rd. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours in affected areas. The heavy and flooding rains was expected to delay the spring planting on farms. Garden center and nursery business which was already slow because of the unseasonably cold weather was dealt another set-back. Twelve counties in the state were declared federal disaster areas and included Burlington, Camden, Gloucester, Mercer, Middlesex, Morris and Somerset Counties.   |Rain began falling throughout the area during the late evening on the 14th. It started briefly as snow over some of the higher terrain of Susse County. The rain fell at its heaviest from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th to about 3 a.m. EDT on the 16th. As the upper air disturbance moved across New Jersey, the rain did change to snow for a couple of hours around sunrise on the 16th and accumulated about an inch or two on grassy areas in western New Jersey. The steady precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening on the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches. Event snowfall totals were an inch or two in most areas, mainly on grassy areas. |The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. The combination of the heavy rain, even some snow and the winds helped knock down numerous trees and power lines. Peak wind gusts averaged between 40 and 60 mph.|The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: About 125 people were evacuated from the Cedar Crest Mobile Home Park in Vineland on the 15th after the Blackwater Branch Creek flooded. Most were able to return on the 16th. Another eight homes were flooded in Vineland near the Maurice River. Landis Avenue (New Jersey State Route 56) was closed between Rosenhayn and Vineland because of severe damage to the Landis Avenue Bridge at Rainbow Lake. Four roads were closed due to flooding in Maurice River Township. The Petticoat Stream flooded basements in Millville. Weymouth Road near the Willow Grove Lake near Newfield was closed to traffic out of fear of a compromised spillway. The heavy rain damaged farm fields, submerged seeds and young lettuce.|The Maurice River at Norma was above its 4 foot flood stage from 522 a.m. EDT on the 16th through 900 p.m. EDT on the 20th. It crested at 5.83 feet at 900 p.m. EDT on the 18th.|Precipitation totals included 5.56 inches in Upper Deerfield, 4.93 inches in Vineland, 4.00 inches in Newport, 3.00 inches in Bridgeton, 2.75 inches in Shiloh, and 2.50 inches in Leesburg. |

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The nor'easter caused widespread minor with pockets of moderate tidal flooding along Delaware Bay, Raritan Bay and the Atlantic Ocean on the 15th and 16th. It also caused beach erosion. The worst reported tidal flooding occurred along Monmouth County where tidal flooding occurred for up to three high tide cycles. Tidal flooding on the 16th occurred along the westward facing side of Barnegat Bay in Ocean County as strong to high west winds pushed the water east.| |In Monmouth County, the combination of the run-off from the heavy rain and the tides caused many roads to flood including New Jersey State Roads 35 and 36. Municipalities affected by tidal and roadway flooding included Aberdeen, Belford, Belmar, Hazlet, Manasquan, Middletown, Port Monmouth, Sea Bright and Union Beach. In an effort to reduce tidal flooding, water was pumped from Lake Como in Belmar. On the beaches themselves, vertical cuts to the beaches averaged 2 to 4 feet, but reached as high as 6 feet in Sea Bright, Deal and Asbury Park. Cuts to the dune systems themselves occurred in Deal, Long Branch, Monmouth Beach and Sea Bright. The horizontal dune cut in Sea Bright reached 1500 feet.|In Ocean County, the worst reported flooding occurred on the bay side on the 16th. Portions of State Route 35 were flooded. The ramps into and out of State Route 37 in Seaside Heights were flooded. Bay flooding was reported in Lavallette, Mantoloking, Seaside Park and Surf City.|Tidal flooding reached into the yards of homes on Cedar Bonnet Island.|On the Atlantic Ocean side, erosion was severe in Harvey Cedars in the Atlantic Avenue area where the vertical cut reached between 6 and 8 feet. Elsewhere in Harvey Cedars and the county, most of the vertical cuts were between 2 and 4 feet. |In Atlantic County, the only reported erosion was in the Atlantic City area were a cut to the dune system occurred in a 10 foot area. The vertical cut to the beach reached 5 to 6 feet. Damage to the boardwalk railing was also reported.|In Cape May County, The high tide the night of the 15th forced the closure of the George Redding Bridge in Wildwood (New Jersey State Route 47) and Ocean Drive (County Route 619) between Ocean City and Strathmere. Tidal flooding also occurred on the Delaware Bay side with wave damage reported to several homes in Reeds Beach (Middle Township).|The pounding surf also caused the geotubes to become exposed in Strathmere. Most of the vertical cuts were 1 to 3 feet, except for a small section in Cape May City where the vertical cut to the beaches reached 6 feet.|In Cumberland County, in Downe Township, the road to Money Island was breached by the tide and sand was tossed on the road. In Seabreeze (Fairfield Township), a multi-million dollar shore restoration project (that was nearing completion) was badly damaged. In Maurice River Township, tidal flooding along the bay and the Maurice River flooded several roads including Glades and Matts Landing Roads.|The highest tides included 8.13 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) on the morning of the 16th, and 7.22 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County), on the evening of the 15th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water and moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of a lee side trough and an approaching cold front helped trigger strong thunderstorms during the late afternoon of the 28th. This led to the flooding of a few streams across north central Sussex County. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached three inches in Wantage, Frankford and Vernon Townships.

The first heat wave of the season (loosely defined as three consecutive days with high temperatures of 90 degrees or higher) occurred across most of central and southern from the 26th through the 28th. The hottest day with respect to both temperature and humidity occurred on the 27th as many high temperatures reached into the mid 90s and heat indices were as high as around 100F. Heavy rain from the remnants of Tropical Storm Barry caused flash flooding in parts of Northeast New Jersey.  Rainfall amounts ranged from just under 3.25 inches in Rivervale, Bergen County to just over an inch at Hawthorne, Passaic County, with most locations across Northeast New Jersey receiving between 1.5 and 2.5 inches of rainfall. Wind gusts if 30-40 mph produced sporadic instances of minor tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Ridge Road flooded due to flash flooding between Linden Street and Mark Twain Way.

Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail in Newark Metro area in Southeast Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel size hail was reported at Dover Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms produced damaging winds and large hail in Newark Metro area in Southeast Essex County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High winds downed trees and wires across multiple locations within Newark from 8:22 pm until 8:31 pm EDT. The Newark Fire Department reported a tree fell through a house at 40 Dover Street and crashed through a second story bedroom, which resulted in 2 injuries to children. Vehicles were damaged by fallen trees at 40 Dover Street and between 121 and 125 Pine Grove Terrace. Roofs were also damaged between 121 and 125 Pine Grove Terrace. The Newark Airport Automated Surface Observing System measured a peak gust of 48 knots from the north at 8:25 pm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rains associated with a low pressure system which at one time was Tropical Storm Barry fell across eastern New Jersey during the morning of June 4th mainly between 3 a.m. EDT and 9 a.m.EDT. The heavy rain produced widespread flooding in Atlantic County and poor drainage flooding in Ocean and Cape May Counties. The wet roadways also contributed to traffic accidents, especially in Monmouth and Ocean Counties. The only reported fatal accident of the morning occurred in Wall Township along the Garden State Parkway. A male driver made an unsafe lane change, lost control of his vehicle and flipped over.  The low pressure system that was Barry moved from Myrtle Beach, South Carolina on the morning of the 3rd to just east of New Jersey on the morning of the 4th. The heaviest rain fell as the low pressure system made its closest pass to the New Jersey coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totlas included 1.80 inches in Sandy Hook, 1.63 inches in Marlboro and 1.57 inches in Sea Girt.

The combination of a weak onshore flow circulating around a high pressure system over the Canadian Maritimes, the spring tides associated with the new moon and some of the highest astronomical tides of the year helped cause minor tidal flooding during the times of high tide along the New Jersey coast and Delaware and Raritan Bays during the evening of the 13th. Some isolated minor tidal flooding also occurred during the evening of the 14th. Highest tides included 7.20 feet above mean lower low water in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 6.79 feet at Atlantic City (Atlantic County). Minor tidal flooding begins at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass for early June helped trigger some widely scattered severe thunderstorms across the northern half of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A SKYWARN Spotter reported penny size hail near Exit 4 of Interstate 80 north of Columbia.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid air mass for early June helped trigger some widely scattered severe thunderstorms across the northern half of New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down scattered trees and power lines throughout Mercer County. The most concentrated wind damage was in Hopewell, Ewing and Lawrence Townships.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike left a 14-year-old boy unconscious and his shirt on fire in Cherry Hill Township. He was found lying face down on Monroe Avenue. He had no pulse when he was found by emergency personnel. He was revived and taken to Cooper University Hospital and then transferred to the burn center at Temple University Hospital in Philadelphia.
Heavy showers and thunderstorms produced isolated flash flooding in portions of Bergen and Hudson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant road flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike caused a small house fire in Washington Township. The fire caused minor damage to the attic and two apartments below it. At another home around the corner, an additional lightning strike caused damage to the home's electrical lines.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain forced the closure of major roads in Atlantic County. The heaviest rain fell across the eastern part of the county. Cars were stuck in flood waters in Ventnor. Two feet of standing water was common on the streets of Northfield. Road closures occurred in Pleasantville and Somers Point. Precipitation totals included 3.24 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport, 2.97 inches in Atlantic City, 2.52 inches in Forsythe and 2.17 inches in Estell Manor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance that passed through the region on an unseasonably hot and humid day helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down numerous trees, tree limbs and wires across Mercer County. Emergency personnel responded to about 65 weather related calls. The worst reported damage occurred to a Hamilton Township home when an evergreen tree fell through its roof into the living room.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Knowlton Township including one on Ramseyburg Road.

The combination of the fresh water run-off and the onshore flow associated with the nor'easter caused minor tidal flooding at the time of high tide along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries overnight on the 15th. This also slowed the run-off of streams, creeks and rivers that empty into the Delaware River. Flooding along the Admiral Wilson Boulevard along the Cooper River in Camden (Camden County) was tidal related. Tidal flooding also affected sections of U.S. Route 130 in Camden County. In Greenwich Township (Gloucester County), tidal flood waters along the Repaupo Creek were forced into the Delaware River. The high tide at the Delaware River in Philadelphia reached 8.72 feet above mean lower low water at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 16th. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.

A low pressure system moved northeast from the Mid Atlantic States across Northeast New Jersey and Eastern Connecticut on the 27th.  Two bands of heavy rain occurred:  one extended northeast across Hunterdon and Passaic Counties; the other extended from Somerset northeast across Eastern Essex and Eastern Bergen Counties.  Storm total rainfall amounts in these bands ranged from 2 to 4 inches, which resulted in flash flooding across parts of the region.|Storm total rainfall amounts for:|Bergen County - ranged from 2.01 inches at Cliffside Park to 3.26 inches at Teterboro Airport.|Essex County - ranged from 2.36 inches at Newark Airport to 2.40 inches at West Orange.|Hudson County - ranged from 1.46 inches at Jersey City to 2.88 inches at Harrison.|Passaic County - 2.12 inches was measured in West Patterson.|Union County - 2.97 inches was measured in Elizabeth.|A NWS Cooperative Observer reported flash flooding of Route 46 in Lodi, where lanes in both directions were closed, at 4:15 am.|Amateur radio operators reported flash flooding of I-280 in Newark at 5:00 am.|The broadcast media reported flash flooding along portions of:|Route 46 in Wayne, where 2 lanes were closed and Highway 508 in Kearny at 6:00 am.|Route 17 in Rutherford at 9:10 am.|I-280 in Kearny at 9:11 am and in Newark at 9:24 am.|I-78 west bound at Exit 43 (Diamond Hill Road) in Berkeley Heights at 10:14 am.|Route 139 lower level west bound before Tonnelle Avenue in Jersey City at 10:18 am.|Highway 21 south bound at River Drive in Passaic at 10:50 am.

The nor'easter caused widespread minor with pockets of moderate tidal flooding along Delaware Bay, Raritan Bay and the Atlantic Ocean on the 15th and 16th. It also caused beach erosion. The worst reported tidal flooding occurred along Monmouth County where tidal flooding occurred for up to three high tide cycles. Tidal flooding on the 16th occurred along the westward facing side of Barnegat Bay in Ocean County as strong to high west winds pushed the water east.| |In Monmouth County, the combination of the run-off from the heavy rain and the tides caused many roads to flood including New Jersey State Roads 35 and 36. Municipalities affected by tidal and roadway flooding included Aberdeen, Belford, Belmar, Hazlet, Manasquan, Middletown, Port Monmouth, Sea Bright and Union Beach. In an effort to reduce tidal flooding, water was pumped from Lake Como in Belmar. On the beaches themselves, vertical cuts to the beaches averaged 2 to 4 feet, but reached as high as 6 feet in Sea Bright, Deal and Asbury Park. Cuts to the dune systems themselves occurred in Deal, Long Branch, Monmouth Beach and Sea Bright. The horizontal dune cut in Sea Bright reached 1500 feet.|In Ocean County, the worst reported flooding occurred on the bay side on the 16th. Portions of State Route 35 were flooded. The ramps into and out of State Route 37 in Seaside Heights were flooded. Bay flooding was reported in Lavallette, Mantoloking, Seaside Park and Surf City.|Tidal flooding reached into the yards of homes on Cedar Bonnet Island.|On the Atlantic Ocean side, erosion was severe in Harvey Cedars in the Atlantic Avenue area where the vertical cut reached between 6 and 8 feet. Elsewhere in Harvey Cedars and the county, most of the vertical cuts were between 2 and 4 feet. |In Atlantic County, the only reported erosion was in the Atlantic City area were a cut to the dune system occurred in a 10 foot area. The vertical cut to the beach reached 5 to 6 feet. Damage to the boardwalk railing was also reported.|In Cape May County, The high tide the night of the 15th forced the closure of the George Redding Bridge in Wildwood (New Jersey State Route 47) and Ocean Drive (County Route 619) between Ocean City and Strathmere. Tidal flooding also occurred on the Delaware Bay side with wave damage reported to several homes in Reeds Beach (Middle Township).|The pounding surf also caused the geotubes to become exposed in Strathmere. Most of the vertical cuts were 1 to 3 feet, except for a small section in Cape May City where the vertical cut to the beaches reached 6 feet.|In Cumberland County, in Downe Township, the road to Money Island was breached by the tide and sand was tossed on the road. In Seabreeze (Fairfield Township), a multi-million dollar shore restoration project (that was nearing completion) was badly damaged. In Maurice River Township, tidal flooding along the bay and the Maurice River flooded several roads including Glades and Matts Landing Roads.|The highest tides included 8.13 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) on the morning of the 16th, and 7.22 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County), on the evening of the 15th. Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water and moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water.

Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached between 3 and 4 inches in a band from Hillsborough and Branchburg Townships northeast through Somerville, Manville, Bridgewater, Bound Brook, Warren, Green Brook, North Plainfield and Watchung. This caused flash flooding of smaller streams in the early morning of the 27th and led to minor flooding along some of the larger rivers in the county later that day. In Hillsborough Township, a woman was rescued from the Raritan River after she drove around barricades and became caught in a flooded section of Somerset County Route 625 just before the John Basilone Veterans Memorial Bridge leading into Raritan Borough. During the early morning both U.S. Routes 206 and 202 were closed in Raritan Borough. There were a few voluntary evacuations in Green Brook. Two roads were closed.  U.S. Route 22 was among four roads closed in North Plainfield. One home was evacuated on Princeton Lane. In Bound Brook, the worst flooding was reported on Piedmont road, Windsor and Chestnut Streets and Linden Avenue. On the latter road, up to three feet of water was reported in basements. There were road closures also in Hillsborough and Franklin Townships. Six roads were closed in Hillsborough Township and schools had a two hour delayed opening. Northbound Interstate 287 north of milepost 16 near the South Bound Brook and Franklin Township border was closed to passenger vehicles during the early morning. Tractor-trailers were permitted to go through. |The Pike Run at Belle Mead was above its 7 foot flood stage from 717 a.m. through 815 a.m. EDT on the 27th. It crested at 7.21 feet at 745 a.m. EDT. The Stony Brook at North Plainfield was above its 7 foot flood stage from 338 a.m. EDT through 427 a.m. EDT on the 27th. It crested at 7.5 feet at 4 a.m. EDT. Farther downstream, the Stony Brook at Watchung was above its 14.5 foot flood stage from 314 a.m. through 451 a.m. EDT on the 27th. It crested at 15.9 feet at 4 a.m. EDT. The North Branch of the Raritan River at South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 5 a.m. EDT through 716 p.m. EDT on the 27th. The combination of the fresh water run-off and the onshore flow associated with the nor'easter caused minor tidal flooding at the time of high tide along tidal sections of the Delaware River and its tributaries overnight on the 15th. This also slowed the run-off of streams, creeks and rivers that empty into the Delaware River. Flooding along the Admiral Wilson Boulevard along the Cooper River in Camden (Camden County) was tidal related. Tidal flooding also affected sections of U.S. Route 130 in Camden County. In Greenwich Township (Gloucester County), tidal flood waters along the Repaupo Creek were forced into the Delaware River. The high tide at the Delaware River in Philadelphia reached 8.72 feet above mean lower low water at 1230 a.m. EDT on the 16th. Minor tidal flooding begins at 8.2 feet above mean lower low water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to New Jersey that started on the 15th. The worst flooding occurred along the Raritan and Passaic River Basins. It was the worst flooding in the Raritan Basin since Hurricane Floyd in 1999. Bound Brook and Manville were once again hit hard as were communities on the other side of the Raritan River in Middlesex County. Along the Passaic Basin, Lincoln Park also was hit hard. The nor'easter also brought strong to high winds as well as some snow to the state on the 16th. Numerous streams and rivers flooded, but the flooding along the Delaware River was minimal.  |Statewide damage was estimated at $180 million dollars. It was the second worst rain storm (not related to a hurricane) in the state's history. Acting Governor Richard J. Codey declared a state of emergency on the 16th. About 5,000 people were evacuated in 11 of the state's 21 counties. At one time over 70 major roadways and interstates were closed including New Jersey State Routes 20, 23, 38, 46, 70 and 73 as well as U.S. Route 30 and Interstates 80 and 380. There were over 700 traffic accidents indirectly caused by the flooding and heavy rain. Three people in the state drowned. The strong winds caused about 120,000 homes and businesses in the state to lose power. Power was not completely restored until the 18th. The combination of the snow, wind (power outages) and flooding caused many school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings on the 16th. Even Rutgers University was closed on the 16th and 17th. In the Raritan Basin, school closings lasted into the week of April 23rd. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours in affected areas. The heavy and flooding rains was expected to delay the spring planting on farms. Garden center and nursery business which was already slow because of the unseasonably cold weather was dealt another set-back. Twelve counties in the state were declared federal disaster areas and included Burlington, Camden, Gloucester, Mercer, Middlesex, Morris and Somerset Counties.   |Rain began falling throughout the area during the late evening on the 14th. It started briefly as snow over some of the higher terrain of Susse County. The rain fell at its heaviest from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th to about 3 a.m. EDT on the 16th. As the upper air disturbance moved across New Jersey, the rain did change to snow for a couple of hours around sunrise on the 16th and accumulated about an inch or two on grassy areas in western New Jersey. The steady precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening on the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches. Event snowfall totals were an inch or two in most areas, mainly on grassy areas. |The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. The combination of the heavy rain, even some snow and the winds helped knock down numerous trees and power lines. Peak wind gusts averaged between 40 and 60 mph.|The nor'easter low pressure system initially formed in the southern Rockies on the 12th and moved to Arkansas on the morning of the 14th. At 8 p.m. EDT on the 14th, there were two low pressure systems, one in Kentucky and the other in western Alabama. The southern low pressure system became the predominate one overnight and at 8 a.m. EDT on the 15th, it (996 millibar low) was located in western North Carolina. As the 15th continued, it moved northeast and intensified rapidly. At 2 p.m. EDT the nor'easter (989 millibars) was near Raleigh, North Carolina. At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The heavy rain caused vehicles to become stranded in Camden. In Collingswood, U.S. Route 130 was closed in three places. New Jersey State Routes 45 and 70 were flooded. Flooding along the Great Harbor River closed roadways in Winslow Township.|The Great Egg Harbor River at Folsom (Atlantic County) was above its 6 foot flood stage from 210 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 100 a.m. EDT on the 20th. It crested at 8.03 feet at 1045 p.m. EDT on the 17th. The Cooper River at Haddonfield was above its 2.8 foot flood stage from 932 a.m. EDT on the 15th through 455 p.m. EDT on the 16th. It crested at 4.66 feet at 418 a.m. EDT on the 16th.|Precipitation totals included 6.29 inches in Pennsauken and 4.26 inches in Blackwood.|

At 8 p.m. EDT, the nor'easter was a 979 millibar low pressure system near Virginia Beach, Virginia. Another low pressure system formed on its warm front during the afternoon over Chesapeake Bay and at 8 p.m. EDT it (982 millibars) was over extreme southwest New Jersey. The lows consolidated into one again overnight and a very intense 973 millibar low pressure system was located over Monmouth County, New Jersey at 2 a.m. EDT on the 16th. At 8 a.m. EDT on the 16th the nor'easter was down to 968 millibars and located over New York City. That was a drop in central pressure of 28 millibars in 24 hours which qualified it as a meteorological bomb (a drop in central pressure of at least 24 millibars in 24 hours) low pressure system. The nor'easter bottomed at a pressure of about 966 millibars (28.53 inches) at 11 a.m. EDT just south of Long Island, New York. To put into prospective how low the pressure was with this nor'easter, that central pressure is near the border of the pressure defined category 2 and category 3 hurricane once used on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Scale. As the 16th continued, the nor'easter weakened and meandered across southwest New England and the New York and New Jersey coastal waters. By 2 a.m. EDT on the 17th, the nor'easter's central pressure rose to 988 millibars and a second storm formed on the triple point of its fronts south of Nova Scotia. This became the main low pressure system. The low pressure system then slowly moved southeast over the next couple of days. The heaviest precipitation fell as the low pressure system was rapidly intensifying on the 15th into the early part of the 16th. The strongest winds ceased after the low pressure system started to fill on the afternoon of the 16th.

An intense nor'easter brought heavy rain and flooding to New Jersey that started on the 15th. The worst flooding occurred along the Raritan and Passaic River Basins. It was the worst flooding in the Raritan Basin since Hurricane Floyd in 1999. Bound Brook and Manville were once again hit hard as were communities on the other side of the Raritan River in Middlesex County. Along the Passaic Basin, Lincoln Park also was hit hard. The nor'easter also brought strong to high winds as well as some snow to the state on the 16th. Numerous streams and rivers flooded, but the flooding along the Delaware River was minimal.  |Statewide damage was estimated at $180 million dollars. It was the second worst rain storm (not related to a hurricane) in the state's history. Acting Governor Richard J. Codey declared a state of emergency on the 16th. About 5,000 people were evacuated in 11 of the state's 21 counties. At one time over 70 major roadways and interstates were closed including New Jersey State Routes 20, 23, 38, 46, 70 and 73 as well as U.S. Route 30 and Interstates 80 and 380. There were over 700 traffic accidents indirectly caused by the flooding and heavy rain. Three people in the state drowned. The strong winds caused about 120,000 homes and businesses in the state to lose power. Power was not completely restored until the 18th. The combination of the snow, wind (power outages) and flooding caused many school districts to either cancel classes or have delayed openings on the 16th. Even Rutgers University was closed on the 16th and 17th. In the Raritan Basin, school closings lasted into the week of April 23rd. The nor'easter was so nasty that even the Internal Revenue Service extended the tax deadline for 48 hours in affected areas. The heavy and flooding rains was expected to delay the spring planting on farms. Garden center and nursery business which was already slow because of the unseasonably cold weather was dealt another set-back. Twelve counties in the state were declared federal disaster areas and included Burlington, Camden, Gloucester, Mercer, Middlesex, Morris and Somerset Counties.   |Rain began falling throughout the area during the late evening on the 14th. It started briefly as snow over some of the higher terrain of Susse County. The rain fell at its heaviest from about 3 a.m. EDT on the 15th to about 3 a.m. EDT on the 16th. As the upper air disturbance moved across New Jersey, the rain did change to snow for a couple of hours around sunrise on the 16th and accumulated about an inch or two on grassy areas in western New Jersey. The steady precipitation ended during the afternoon and early evening on the 16th. Event precipitation totals averaged 3 to 6 inches. Event snowfall totals were an inch or two in most areas, mainly on grassy areas. |The strongest winds occurred as the nor'easter pulled northeast of the region on the 16th from the early morning into the afternoon. One lane of U.S. Route 322 was also closed.  |The Great Egg Harbor River at Folsom was above its 6 foot flood stage from 210 p.m. EDT on the 16th through 100 a.m. EDT on the 20th. One of the hottest and most humid air masses of the summer affected the central and southern New Jersey on the 7th and most of the state on the 8th. Highest temperatures were close to 100 degrees in most areas. The 8th was the hottest day. In addition, dew point temperatures, especially on the 8th were as high as the mid to upper 70s. Low pressure was located in southeastern Canada during the early morning hours of August 8th. A cold front associated with this area of low pressure was moving across the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley regions during the morning. Well out ahead of this cold front was a surface trough which moved through the Mid Atlantic region during the very early morning hours on August 8th. A very warm and humid air mass was in place, and with some upper-level energy, a round of showers and thunderstorms moved through parts of eastern Pennsylvania and northern New Jersey. Some of the thunderstorms turned locally severe between 2:20 AM and 5:40 AM EDT with damaging winds. Locally heavy rainfall also occurred, which resulted in some localized urban flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm caused wind damage across Hunterdon County from Bloomsbury Borough east through Readington Township. A National Weather Service storm survey concluded that the worst wind damage occurred in Bethlehem Township where wind gusts were estimated to reach 70 mph. The hardest hit areas in the township were near the intersection of Rounsville and White Roads as well as Trush Lane. Numerous trees were uprooted and snapped. Dozens of trees were uprooted on one property. Minor damage occurred to a couple of homes as trees fell on them. A fallen tree also damaged a swimming pool and vehicle. In Tewksbury Township, County Routes 517 and 523 were closed. The Oldwick Section of the township completely lost power. About 10,000 homes and businesses lost power in the county, power was fully restored the evening of the 8th. ||

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A forest fire started in Wharton State Forest in Washington Township on the afternoon of August 3rd. It burned 2,443 acres in Washington and Shamong Townships before it was contained. It was spotted in the air by a New Jersey Forestry Service helicopter at 250 p.m. EDT. It started near a dirt road about a mile off of U.S. Route 206 in Washington Township and was believed to have been the work of an arsonist. The fire jumped the Mullica River and spread into Shamong Township quickly. No homes, camping or recreational facilities were threatened. But, U.S. Route 206 was sporadically closed from the 3rd into the morning of the 5th between U.S. Route 30 in Atlantic County and either Tuckertown Road (County Route 620) or County Route 541 in Burlington County. The fire briefly jumped across U.S. Route 206 near Atsion Lake in Shamong Township. As a precaution, the Atsion Lake recreational area, the Mullica and Lower Forge Camping areas and canoeing sites on the Mullica and Batsto Rivers were closed until the fire was completely contained. The fire was contained at 8 a.m. EDT on the 6th. About an inch of rain that fell overnight assisted the fire fighters. No injuries or property damage was reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm blasted across a portion of Branchburg Township at 4:25 AM EDT on the 8th. A National Weather Service storm survey indicated that damaging straight line winds of up to 70 mph occurred. These damaging winds downed power poles and numerous trees. Some of the tree tops were completely sheared off. On one property in the North Branch area of the township, 100 to 200 year old trees were split apart. Quarry Lane in the Martinsville section of the township was closed to traffic because of downed trees. On another property, a gazebo was just tossed by the damaging winds. Wind damage cut across the central part of the county into Bridgewater and Franklin Townships where several more trees and power lines were knocked down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm dropped a swath of hail across central Hunterdon County. The largest hail (golf ball size) fell in Frenchtown Borough. Quarter size hail fell in Raritan Township. Penny size hail fell in Clinton and Flemington. The worst reported crop damage from the large hail occurred in Pittstown (Franklin Township). Hail bruised about 80 percent of the tomato crop that the 17th annual Great Tomato Tasting Contest was cancelled.

The combination of an onshore flow during the day on the 10th and higher astronomical spring tides associated with the approaching new moon caused pockets of minor tidal flooding at the time of the evening high tide. The high tide reached 6.8 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.7 feet above mean lower low water in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. A weak low pressure system that moved through New Jersey on the 10th helped establish the weak onshore flow prior to the arrival of the high tide.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down over 120 trees in Medford and Evesham Townships. The Hoot Owl section of Medford Township was hit particularly hard. Ten homes suffered moderate damage. About another 50 more suffered minor damage in both the Hoot Owl and Wood Ridge sections. Trees as large as two feet in diameter and 80 feet in height were knocked down. It was estimated that it would take two to three weeks to haul away all of the debris. In Evesham Township, there were 20 calls for downed trees. Three trees caused home damage. Power lines were also knocked down along New Jersey State Route 73. About 1,000 homes and businesses lost power in the two townships. It was restored to all places by the morning of the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northeast onshore flow that preceded the low pressure system which at one time was Tropical Storm Barry produced minor tidal flooding at the times of high tide during the evening of the 3rd. The high tide reached 6.82 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.78 feet above mean lower low water in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding begins at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. The low pressure system that was Barry moved from Myrtle Beach, South Carolina on the morning of the 3rd to just east of New Jersey on the morning of the 4th. The onshore flow ended that morning and no additional coastal flooding occurred.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few large tree limbs and wires in Whitehouse Station (Readington Township).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance that passed through the region on an unseasonably hot and humid day helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in White Township including on New Jersey State Route 47.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The northeast onshore flow that preceded the low pressure system which at one time was Tropical Storm Barry produced minor tidal flooding at the times of high tide during the evening of the 3rd. The high tide reached 6.82 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.78 feet above mean lower low water in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding begins at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. The low pressure system that was Barry moved from Myrtle Beach, South Carolina on the morning of the 3rd to just east of New Jersey on the morning of the 4th. The onshore flow ended that morning and no additional coastal flooding occurred.

An unseasonably warm and humid airmass was over the Mid Atlantic region during the 19th. A strong area of low pressure moved into southeastern Canada during the afternoon, and a strong cold front associated with it approached and moved through the Mid Atlantic and Northeast in the evening. As a result, numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms developed. There was plenty of wind energy above the surface, therefore some of the isolated thunderstorms and even heavier showers produced strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked over several trees in Vineland. The downed trees damaged a couple of vehicles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Lightning from a thunderstorm struck five separate homes in Washington Township, a shed and another home in Franklin Township and caused several brush fires in the two townships. The worst reported damage occurred to a home on Jacqueline Place (Franklin Township) that had its chimney destroyed by a lightning strike. Downed wires (by lightning) also caused several traffic light outages. No serious injuries were reported.

A weak upper air disturbance combined with an unseasonably hot and humid air mass to trigger pulse type severe thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 10th in New Jersey. In addition to the severe weather, the thunderstorms were prolific cloud-to-ground lightning producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Franklin and Monroe Townships. In Franklin Township, trees were knocked down along County Route 659 near County Route 538. In Monroe Township, trees were knocked down along Blue Bell Road and Corkery Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A high pressure system that remained over the Delmarva Peninsula for the third consecutive morning caused widespread dense fog to form across the region. Dense fog formed in all areas around midnight EDT on the 6th and took until around 10 a.m. EDT to dissipate.

The combination of the onshore circulation around a high pressure system over New England and the Canadian Maritimes and spring tides associated with the full moon caused widespread minor tidal flooding during the morning high tides on the 26th and 27th. Less widespread minor tidal flooding also occurred during the two high tides on the 25th. Tidal flooding extended into Raritan and Delaware Bays. The highest tides occurred on the morning of the 26th in the southern part of the state and on the morning of the 27th in the northern part of the state. tidal flooding was exacerbated by ongoing rain during the high tide on the 27th. The highest tides included 7.44 feet above mean lower low water at Cape May (Cape May County), 7.16 feet above mean lower low water at Sandy Hook (Monmouth County) and 6.7 feet above mean lower low water at Atlantic City (Atlantic County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water, while moderate tidal flooding starts at 7.7 feet above mean lower low water. An offshore flow developed later in the day on the 27th and prevented further tidal flooding.
A strong low pressure system moved northeast through New England and produced strong gusty winds that caused tree damage in Passaic County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Gusty winds knocked down trees and snapped power poles across Passaic County, including Passaic, Haledon, and Clifton.

A slow moving weather pattern gave New Jersey four days of rainy weather which culminated with periods of heavy rain during the first half of the day on the 27th. Four day storm totals averaged 1 to 5 inches across the state with the highest amounts in Morris, Hunterdon, Somerset and Mercer Counties. This caused poor drainage flooding as well as flooding of some of the branches of the Raritan River in Somerset and Hunterdon Counties. Specific storm totals included 4.86 inches in Flemington (Hunterdon County), 4.72 inches in Pottersville (Somerset County), 4.56 inches in Ironia (Morris County), 3.91 inches in Somerdale (Camden County), 3.20 inches in Willingboro (Burlington County), 3.14 inches in Belvidere (Warren County), 3.13 inches at Cape May Court House (Cape May County), 2.98 inches in Andover (Sussex County), 2.91 inches in Trenton (Mercer County), 2.87 inches in West Deptford (Gloucester County), 2.53 inches in Millville (Cumberland County), 2.45 inches at the Atlantic City International Airport (Atlantic County), 2.26 inches in Deans (Middlesex County), 1.82 inches in Cream Ridge (Monmouth County) and 1.28 inches in Barnegat (Ocean County.||The heavy rain was caused by a long and continuous fetch of tropical moisture between a slow moving upper level low from the south central part of the United States and a blocking ridge over the western Atlantic. The closed system lifted into the Great Lakes on Saturday the 27th and permitted a cold front to move through the region that day and shut off the moisture connection from the tropics.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The North Branch of the Raritan River at the borough of South Branch was above its 7 foot flood stage from 1233 p.m. EDT through 1045 p.m. EDT on  the 27th. It crested at 8.35 feet at 8 p.m. EDT. Storm totals within the county since precipitation started falling on Wednesday the 24th included 4.72 inches in Pottersville, 3.28 inches in Belle Mead, 3.13 inches in Somerville, 3.12 inches in Far Hills and 3.04 inches in Skillman.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm badly damaged trees in the Jockey Hollow Encampment area within Mendham Township. About thirty to forty trees were either snapped or uprooted in the area. Four buildings (two log cabins, two pavilions) suffered missile damage from fallen branches penetrating the structures. There were no injuries as the camping area was not in use. The same severe thunderstorm caused wind damage in adjoining Morris Township. Trees were knocked down on Harter Road and Frederick Place. Downed trees blocked one resident's driveway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A cooperative observer estimated wind gusts to around 60 mph as a severe thunderstorm moved through Estell Manor. A mesonet site also reported a wind gust to 54 mph in the municipality.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wet microburst caused wind damage at the Woodbine Municipal Airport within the borough. Several hangers and aircraft were damaged. No injuries were reported. A couple of trees and large tree limbs were also knocked down in the borough. Wind gusts at the airport were estimated to reach 75 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm produced high winds that downed trees from Garwood through Cranford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down large tree limbs and wires in Oceanport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was located in southeastern Canada during the early morning hours of August 8th. A cold front associated with this area of low pressure was moving across the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley regions during the morning. Well out ahead of this cold front was a surface trough which moved through the Mid Atlantic region during the very early morning hours on August 8th. A very warm and humid air mass was in place, and with some upper-level energy, a round of showers and thunderstorms moved through parts of northern New Jersey. Some of the thunderstorms produced torrential downpours and caused poor drainage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms which also produced torrential downpours caused flash flooding in Morristown in and near the Whippany Creek. Seven vehicles (two completely submerged) and basements sustained flood damage. Basements on Cobb Place were flooded. Three roadways within the town were closed. The heavy rain also helped a tree on Olyphant Drive topple onto two vehicles.||The Whippany Creek at Moorestown was above its 6 foot flood stage from 513 a.m. EDT through 603 a.m. EDT on the 8th. It crested at 6.35 feet at 530 a.m. EDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure riding along a warm front produced thunderstorms with heavy rain causing flooding in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: US Route 22 closure both ways between Hillside and Newark due to flooding between N. Broad St. and Frelinghuysen Ave.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper air disturbance in the northwest flow behind the cold front still had sufficient moisture to develop strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania and New Jersey during the afternoon and the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 45-year-old woman was struck by lightning as she was about to enter a restaurant on U.S. Route 9 North in Howell Township. She was pronounced dead about one hour later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of heavy rain caused by an inverted trof associated with a low pressure system that developed southeast of the state fell across northern New Jersey during the morning and afternoon on the 23rd. Storm totals within this band averaged two to three inches and caused poor drainage flooding. The band extended from the Warren/Sussex County border southwest through southern Morris County and northern Somerset County as well as across northeast Middlesex County and the northern half of Monmouth County. The worst reported roadway flooding occurred along New Jersey State Route 36 in Middletown (Monmouth County). The heavy rain caused lane closures on the roadway.||Actual storm totals included 3.34 inches in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 2.76 inches in Walpack (Sussex County), 2.73 inches in Middletown (Monmouth County), 2.53 inches in Randolph (Morris County), 2.45 inches in Hackettstown and Hope (Warren County), 2.44 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 2.41 inches in Basking Ridge (Somerset County), 2.31 inches in Hopatcong (Sussex County), 2.29 inches in Wharton (Morris County), 2.28 inches in Parsippany (Morris County), 2.22 inches in Pequest (Warren County) and 1.59 inches in East Brunswick (Middlesex County).

Well out ahead of this cold front was a surface trough which moved through the Mid Atlantic region during the very early morning hours on August 8th. A very warm and humid air mass was in place, and with some upper-level energy, a round of showers and thunderstorms moved through parts of northern New Jersey. Some of the thunderstorms produced torrential downpours and caused poor drainage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with torrential downpours produced flash flooding in Washington Township and Washington Borough. A road (County Route 640) was washed out near Cemetery Hill Road in Washington Township. In Washington Borough up to six feet of water accumulated in basments. Two feet of water amassed on parts of New Jersey State Routes 57 and 31. Businesses had up to two feet of water. A small creek flood helped collapse a swimming pool, knocked over a shed and toppled one man to the ground. Craters formed near Memorial Elementary School.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure was located in southeastern Canada during the early morning hours of August 8th. A cold front associated with this area of low pressure was moving across the Great Lakes and Ohio Valley regions during the morning. Well out ahead of this cold front was a surface trough which moved through the Mid Atlantic region during the very early morning hours on August 8th. A very warm and humid air mass was in place, and with some upper-level energy, a round of showers and thunderstorms moved through parts of northern New Jersey. Some of the thunderstorms produced torrential downpours and caused poor drainage and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from a thunderstorm, approximately 1 to 2 inches, in a short period of time produced urban flooding in Metuchen around 5:20 AM EDT on the 8th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure riding along a warm front produced thunderstorms with heavy rain causing flooding in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Street and Oak Street closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary acted as a focusing mechanism for thunderstorms throughout the day on the 29th in New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tore down a couple of trees across central and northern Middlesex County. A tree was knocked down on Main Street in Spotswood Borough. One tree was knocked down onto a vehicle in East Brunswick Township. A couple of large tree limbs and wires were knocked down in Woodbridge Township. About 13,000 homes and businesses lost power throughout Public Service Electric and Gas's service area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that was approaching northern New Jersey from the Saint Lawrence River Valley initiated severe thunderstorms northwest of the state. These storms maintained their severity as they moved through the northern part of the state during the evening of the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in Mansfield Township.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees and caused isolated power outages in Branchburg Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that was approaching northern New Jersey from the Saint Lawrence River Valley initiated severe thunderstorms northwest of the state. These storms maintained their severity as they moved through the northern part of the state during the evening of the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down, toppled, uprooted and twisted numerous trees in Readington Township from Cole to Roosevelt Roads. The trees were knocked down in different directions suggesting a pretty strong wet microburst occurred within the township. No homes or buildings were damaged.

Some of the thunderstorms turned locally severe and also produced lightning damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Frequent cloud to ground lightning associated with a thunderstorm that moved through Metuchen at 5:20 AM EDT on the 8th struck a house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning struck the shed of a Tewksbury Township home. A male in a nearby house complained of hearing loss after the lightning struck. Another lightning strike caused an abandoned house fire on Frontage Road in Franklin Township. The house was fully engulfed when fire fighters arrived. The house was planned on being razed for an affordable housing development.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably hot and humid air mass affected most of New Jersey on the 25th. While high temperatures were mainly in the lower to mid 90s inland, dew point temperatures averaged in the mid 70s. This produced afternoon heat indices of around 105F which were second only to August 8th as the highest of the summer. A cold front moved through the area early on the 26th and limited this event to only one day.||Highest temperatures included 94 degrees in Woodstown (Salem County), Pennsauken (Camden County), Hammonton and Estell Manor (Atlantic County) and Mount Laurel (Burlington County), 93 degrees in Millville (Cumberland County), Tuckahoe (Cape May County), Clayton (Gloucester County), Deans (Middlesex County), Flemington (Hunterdon County), West Deptford (Gloucester County) and Trenton (Mercer County), Berkeley Township (Ocean County), 92 degrees in Hillsborough (Somerset County), Cream Ridge (Monmouth County) and Kingwood (Hunterdon County) and 91 degrees at the Atlantic City International Airport.

A band of heavy rain caused by an inverted trof associated with a low pressure system that developed southeast of the state fell across northern New Jersey during the morning and afternoon on the 23rd. Storm totals within this band averaged two to three inches and caused poor drainage flooding. The band extended from the Warren/Sussex County border southwest through southern Morris County and northern Somerset County as well as across northeast Middlesex County and the northern half of Monmouth County. The worst reported roadway flooding occurred along New Jersey State Route 36 in Middletown (Monmouth County). The heavy rain caused lane closures on the roadway.||Actual storm totals included 3.34 inches in Keansburg (Monmouth County), 2.76 inches in Walpack (Sussex County), 2.73 inches in Middletown (Monmouth County), 2.53 inches in Randolph (Morris County), 2.45 inches in Hackettstown and Hope (Warren County), 2.44 inches in Blairstown (Warren County), 2.41 inches in Basking Ridge (Somerset County), 2.31 inches in Hopatcong (Sussex County), 2.29 inches in Wharton (Morris County), 2.28 inches in Parsippany (Morris County), 2.22 inches in Pequest (Warren County) and 1.59 inches in East Brunswick (Middlesex County).

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down two large trees onto County Route 517 in Vernon Township near the New York border. A tree was also knocked down onto a car in Barry Lakes.

A cold front that was approaching northern New Jersey from the Saint Lawrence Valley initiated thunderstorms northwest of the state. These storms caused lightning damage as they moved through Somerset County late in the evening on the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Lightning and the ensuing fire damaged a Bernardsville Borough home on Ballantine Road. The roof was ablaze when firefighters arrived at the scene. The fire caused water and smoke damage to the roof as well as an upper level multi-purpose room and bathroom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that was approaching northern New Jersey from the Saint Lawrence Valley initiated thunderstorms northwest of the state. These storms caused lightning damage as they moved through Somerset County late in the evening on the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A lightning strike and ensuing fire totally destroyed one Franklin Township home on Viscaya Court. Residents inside the home escaped unharmed. The fire also damaged the vinyl siding of four nearby homes. One firefighter was injured battling the blaze. Lightning strikes also caused widely scattered power outages throughout the county, mainly in the central part.
