EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Skywarn spotter reported penny size hail fell in Chester Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper air disturbance in the northwest flow behind the cold front still had sufficient moisture to develop strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania and New Jersey during the afternoon and the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down several trees and wires from Freehold Township southeast through Manasquan Borough in Monmouth County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper air disturbance in the northwest flow behind the cold front still had sufficient moisture to develop strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania and New Jersey during the afternoon and the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nickel to quarter size hail fell in Oakhurst Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper air disturbance in the northwest flow behind the cold front still had sufficient moisture to develop strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania and New Jersey during the afternoon and the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An unoccupied at the time two-story home's roof was struck by a bolt of lightning in Middletown Township. The bolt started a fire in the attic area of the home. Damage was described as moderate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The combination of an onshore flow during the day on the 10th and higher astronomical spring tides associated with the approaching new moon caused pockets of minor tidal flooding at the time of the evening high tide. The high tide reached 6.8 feet above mean lower low water in Cape May (Cape May County) and 6.7 feet above mean lower low water in Sandy Hook (Monmouth County). Minor tidal flooding starts at 6.7 feet above mean lower low water. A weak low pressure system that moved through New Jersey on the 10th helped establish the weak onshore flow prior to the arrival of the high tide.
A nearly stationary frontal boundary acted as a focus for a few severe thunderstorms and heavy rain across the central part of New Jersey during the day on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms with heavy rain caused poor drainage flooding across western Monmouth county from Hazlet Township southwest through Millstone Township. The worst reported flooding was in Hazlet along both New Jersey State Routes 35 and 36. Doppler Radar storm total estimates reached around two inches in western Monmouth County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unseasonably warm and humid airmass was over the Mid Atlantic region during the 19th. A strong area of low pressure moved into southeastern Canada during the afternoon, and a strong cold front associated with it approached and moved through the Mid Atlantic and Northeast in the evening. As a result, numerous showers and isolated thunderstorms developed. There was plenty of wind energy above the surface, therefore some of the isolated thunderstorms and even heavier showers produced strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A squall line of strong to severe thunderstorm tore down several trees throughout Hunterdon County. The worst reported damage was in East Amwell Township. The east and west walls of a new barn under construction collapsed. On Deer Run Farm near Clover Hill on Amwell Road, many trees were snapped or knocked down. One downed tree damaged a pickup truck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary frontal boundary acted as a focus for a few severe thunderstorms and heavy rain across the central part of New Jersey during the day on the 18th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In addition to the severe weather, the heavy rain that also fell from the thunderstorms caused poor drainage flooding in eastern Ocean County on the afternoon of the 18th. On Long Beach Island in Ship Bottom, Central Avenue was closed near its intersection with New Jersey State Route 72. One vehicle became stuck in a flooded jughandle off of New Jersey State Route 37 in Toms River. A Skywarn Spotter from Bayville had a storm total of 3.00 inches during that afternoon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved across the eastern Great Lakes region during the morning of August 25th. A cold front associated with this low pressure system was located in the Ohio Valley. A hot and especially humid airmass was found ahead of this front across the Middle Atlantic region on August 25th. As the front approached, showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. The actual cold front however did not clear the region until the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms moved across Warren County during the evening of the 25th. Lightning produced from these thunderstorms caused 6,000 JCP&L customers to lose power in Warren and Hunterdon Counties.

As the front approached, showers and thunderstorms developed and moved across the area. The actual cold front however did not clear the region until the morning of the 26th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of thunderstorms moved across Warren County during the evening of the 25th. Lightning produced from these thunderstorms caused 6,000 JCP&L customers to lose power in Warren and Hunterdon Counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees in extreme southern Warren County including Franklin Township.

There was plenty of wind energy above the surface, therefore some of the isolated thunderstorms and even heavier showers produced strong wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a tree into a home in Lawrence Township. Another tree was knocked down in East Windsor Township. No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Light freezing rain fell across the higher terrain of Sussex County during the second half of the afternoon and into the early evening on the 11th. Temperatures continued to rise during the late afternoon sand early evening and all were above freezing by 7 p.m. EST. Ice accretions were generally trace amounts. Untreated roadways were slippery.
Lightning struck and killed a 47 year old male while fishing on Upper Greenwood Lake.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced lightning that struck a 47 year old male while fishing in a boat over Upper Greenwood Lake in Passaic County, NJ.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An approaching cold front helped trigger some isolated thunderstorms across northwest New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A father and his daughter were struck and injured by lightning while boating on Lake Hopatcong near Brady Bridge. They were heading for shelter when the father was struck on the top of his head. Burn marks were found. He was in critical, but stable condition. His 8-year-old daughter was struck while hiding under the driver's console. Her injuries were not as serious. The thunderstorm was not a prolific cloud-to-ground lightning producer, there were only 6 recorded cloud-to-ground or cloud-to-water strikes in and around Lake Hopatcong.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front helped trigger a severe thunderstorm over eastern Atlantic County. A trained Skywarn Spotter reported penny size hail fell in Brigantine. Dime size hail fell in Atlantic City.
An approaching cold front, spawned thunderstorms that produced heavy rainfall and caused flash flooding across Northeast New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Widespread significant flash flooding resulted in many road closures.|Just south of Ridgewood, the Franklin Avenue underpass at Wolsey Square was closed at 6:05 am.  At 6:06 am, the Maple Avenue underpass was closed near Glen Rock.  Just south of Westwood, the 2 right lanes along Route 17 north were closed at 6:15 am.  At Bergenfield, Route 208 south at Virginia Avenue was closed at 6:20 am.  Flash flooding continued through 7:30 am at many locations throughout Bergen County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Sussex County was on the northwest fringe of the precipitation shield associated with a coastal low pressure system during the night of the 26th. The higher terrain locations failed to rise above freezing as shots of light precipitation moved through. While it was warm enough aloft for precipitation to fall as rain, it froze on contact across some of the higher terrain locations in Sussex County. Since precipitation amounts were light, ice accretions were mostly just a trace. But, untreated walkways and roadways were slippery.

Thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front the afternoon of the 16th...with 2 particularly potent bow echoes. One had a microburst which created damage across parts of Bergen, and Passaic Counties.  The second moved through Essex, Hudson, and Union Counties slightly later in the afternoon, and also had a microburst associated with it, though weaker than that associated with the first bow echo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance helped cause a few severe thunderstorms across northern New Jersey.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Vernon Township.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy showers and thunderstorms produced isolated flash flooding in portions of Bergen and Hudson Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant road flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front that was approaching northern New Jersey from the Saint Lawrence River Valley initiated severe thunderstorms northwest of the state. These storms maintained their severity as they moved through the northern part of the state during the evening of the 3rd.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a couple of trees in Hopatcong Borough. A couple of trees and wires were also tore down in Stanhope Borough. One downed tree fell across Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper air disturbance combined with an unseasonably hot and humid air mass to trigger pulse type severe thunderstorms during the afternoon of the 10th in New Jersey. In addition to the severe weather, the thunderstorms were prolific cloud-to-ground lightning producers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knocked down a few trees and wires in Shamong Township. One vehicle was damaged by a downed tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A vigorous upper air disturbance in the northwest flow behind the cold front still had sufficient moisture to develop strong to severe thunderstorms across Pennsylvania and New Jersey during the afternoon and the evening of the 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm knoced down a few trees and wires in Monmouth Beach Borough.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper air disturbance that passed through the region on an unseasonably hot and humid day helped produce strong to severe thunderstorms across New Jersey during the afternoon and evening of the 27th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The last of five severe thunderstorms to affect Sussex County on the 27th tore down large tree limbs and wires in Wantage Township.
